title: Command Responsibility
subtitle: A Different Perspective — Facts by a Jewish Camp Official
author: Hermann Wenkart
edition: 2024 Edition, edited and with a foreword by Michael Marcovici

parts.2: Historical Analysis — Journal of Holocaust Research, Haifa
parts.3: The Dęblin Camp — Translated Wikipedia Article

blocks.text: This is the story of my family, more specifically the story of my mother Ruth’s early childhood, whose fate in Deblin plays a key role in this book. After the war, my mother came to Vienna with Hermann Wenkart, whom I unfortunately only briefly knew as my grandfather, and his wife Klara, my grandmother. She was raised by the Wenkarts in Vienna, who remained in Austria after the war. Hermann Wenkart passed away in 1974.

In 1959, Ruth met my father, Raphael Marcovici, in Vienna. My father, himself a Holocaust survivor, was deported to Auschwitz from Transylvania, Romania, in 1944, along with his sister Blanca. Blanca was murdered in Auschwitz at the end of 1944. In January 1945, my father was transferred to Dora-Mittelbau, where he was liberated by American troops on April 11, 1945. The rest of my father’s family—his parents, two other sisters, and three brothers—fled to Canada and Israel.

My parents stayed in Vienna and built a supermarket chain together, which they ran until my father passed away in 1982. My sister Annette was born in 1963 and tragically died at the young age of 29. I was born in 1969, work in mechanical engineering, and also live in Vienna.

It took my mother many years after the war to reconnect with relatives, as there was no contact with extended family. Eventually, in 1995, she succeeded in locating her half-brother Paul in Paris, where her biological father had fled during the war. Sadly, Paul had already passed away five years earlier.

From her immediate family, only her aunt Clara, who later adopted her, and her father survived the Holocaust. Her grandfather Emanuel, her sister Hedwig and her young daughter, as well as her mother Anna, were all murdered.
My mother, Ruth Marcovici, passed away on August 2, 2024, in Vienna. She spent her final years at the Jewish retirement home Maimonides in Vienna.

In May 2013, I decided to republish my grandfather’s 1963 book and, in October 2024, to release an expanded edition with added visual material. In 2013, it had become difficult to find copies of the original book, which my mother had often lent to friends to read. Now, in 2024, interest in testimonials from that time remains relevant. I wish to make the book and its message of reconciliation and the story of one of the “Righteous Among the Nations” accessible to a wider audience.

The story my grandfather tells in this book is one of hope, where, amid the greatest catastrophe faced by the Jews, a miracle or a unique coincidence occurred in a camp in Deblin. An Austrian officer risked his life to save 400 Viennese Jews. This story parallels the survival of my uncles and aunts in Romania, who were saved by Christians from the village of Budest and hidden in the forest for years. These two stories have deeply influenced me, and I hope that sharing this book will inspire others with this positive message of humanity.
Michael Marcovici, Vienna, 2024

chapters.title: Back Cover Text of the 1963 Edition

blocks.text: This factual report recounts personally experienced situations, concisely presenting the history of the first deportation transport from Vienna to Opole in the General Government in January 1940, marking the beginning of the resettlement of Vienna’s Jewish community into the Final Solution.

Apart from the many existing narratives of the bloody history from the East during the time of the Thousand-Year Reich, this documentation seeks to honor those individuals who had enough courage to remain humane in the inferno of a world full of brutality! Unbelievable experiences from a time of seemingly unimaginable persecution of people, simply because they did not conform to the ruling regime, are meant to prove that the frequently misused term “command necessity” was conveniently employed as an excuse to diminish guilt during war crimes trials.

Anyone who truly felt and thought humanely could, without particular danger to themselves, redirect commands they received—commands recognized as criminal in their consequences—so that they became ineffective or their impact was mitigated for those affected.

Anyone familiar with the conditions in the extermination zones will be astonished that, in this sea of murderous intent, violence, and brutality, a semi-humane oasis could exist for years, solely because certain individuals, guided by their conscience, acted differently than the criminal regime had intended. Unfortunately, these individuals were very few; had there been more, many millions of innocent victims might not have perished.

The author has chosen only particularly striking episodes that, in their vividness, will keep readers captivated from start to finish, bringing the past to life. Especially for inquisitive young people who approach the past—an era they did not experience themselves—with curiosity, this historical account can answer many questions their fathers might prefer to hide, often out of guilt.
Hermann Wenkart, Vienna, 1963
The topic addressed in this factual report has been presented to the public so frequently since the collapse of the Third Reich that it might no longer arouse interest.

Nonetheless, I decided to write it because of the defense strategy employed in many Nazi war crimes trials, revolving around the concept of “command necessity.” This term was intended to justify irresponsible acts filled with the most atrocious inhumanity, heinous crimes, and incomprehensible ruthlessness devoid of any humanity, to spare the accused from conviction or diminish their guilt.

I followed and even attended some of these trials, astonished by the confidence, even the nonchalance, displayed by those accused. One might have expected at least a shred of guilt or some acknowledgment of their overwhelming culpability to affect their demeanor. Yet, the accused readily hid behind this artificially inflated concept, using it to numb their conscience and downplay their involvement in these crimes.

This report aims to prove that a bit of personal courage, some civil bravery, and the will to act could suffice to avoid mindless obedience and complicity in crimes for which one might later be held accountable. Through clever adaptation and apparent compliance, much could be prevented or at least mitigated for those affected, without facing the risks of defying orders or committing sabotage.

Countless reports detail the overly eager execution of orders, exceeding quotas, and particularly the incomprehensible negative behavior of individuals in key command positions. These accounts starkly contrast with my report, which includes people who, despite immense challenges, managed to uphold humanity and treat Jews humanely. These individuals were not limited to opponents of the regime; even a covert party member with a membership number in the 100,000s risked their life to hide over 100 Jews from deportation, treated them humanely, and eased the burden of forced labor under their command. Civilian officials in the Luftwaffe’s construction office and officers and soldiers at the Deblin airbase, who might be most eligible for a claim of “command necessity,” had the courage not to become accomplices to sadistic criminals.

Just as I reject the collective guilt of Jews exploited by the Nazis, I must also, as a result of my experiences, reject collective accusations in general—whether they were directed at Germans in the past, or Russians and Warsaw Pact nations today. It is always the leaders of a dictatorship and their willing accomplices who spread such misery and commit crimes against humanity. This is why I have written these lines, and if I am criticized for coming forward so late, I can only justify myself by saying that I waited to gain the necessary distance and to report with full human objectivity. It is never too late to tell the truth, especially when taking a just stance.

On a personal note, as is evident in this report, if the term “command necessity” were ever valid, it could rightfully apply to me without arrogance. I am grateful that, thanks to many fortunate coincidences—and not least my own initiative—I was able to act in ways that proved helpful to those affected.

There is much talk of finally coming to terms with the past, of creating an atmosphere of forgiveness by downplaying the unimaginable degradation of human behavior. But this can only happen if a complete reckoning is made. The truly guilty, many of whom have unfortunately evaded justice, must be swiftly brought before impartial courts. In my view, many trials were initiated too late due to the sluggishness of the justice system, giving skilled defense lawyers opportunities to undermine witness testimonies. The passage of time created gaps in memory, casting the actions of the accused in a softened, conciliatory light.

Only a clear distancing from the monstrous atrocities of the Thousand-Year Reich led by Himmler and his ilk will demonstrate to future German generations that they have overcome the inhumanity of the -year Nazi regime. This could also help stifle the resurgence of those clinging to outdated ideologies.

The insistence on “command necessity” by the accused undoubtedly reflects a desire for justification but also serves to diminish the brutality of a time marked by the cruelest oppression of undesirables.

If judges, perhaps still tied to the past or sympathetic to it, yield to this concept, the next generation—already poorly informed by their parents or educators—will gain a distorted view of this shameful era.

To the victims, it is incomprehensible that these atrocities, carried out under state orders, were committed by individuals whose intelligence, education, and otherwise moral character should have enabled them to assess the criminality of these acts but who were still willing to perpetrate them. This moral failure is beyond understanding or justification.

Equally astounding is the apathy of society, which passively allowed the crimes to occur, and the indifference that enabled a false sense of responsibility, paving the way for an improper evaluation of true guilt. This is partly why those clinging to old ideologies believe they can again hold positions of influence in public and government. I have often had the opportunity to discuss the Nazi era with inquisitive young people. In these discussions, they could not understand why so few people were willing to help those being persecuted. Failing to provide assistance during life-threatening situations is a punishable offense, especially for educated individuals. How many failed to offer such help, and how many contributed instead to the violence?

Time may heal many wounds, but the scale of the crimes, the vast number of murdered individuals, and the enormous suffering of that time carry an obligation to ensure true criminals face justice and opportunistic accomplices are warned. For this reason, the excuse of “command necessity” must not be accepted.

Many incidents described in this report may seem implausible to those familiar with the conditions in occupied Poland. In this sea of violence, blood, and atrocities, it seemed impossible for an island of humanity to exist—where those who felt compassion and risked their own lives managed to create a semblance of survivable conditions. This was especially unique during the war’s final years and should not be withheld from the public.

Giving due recognition to these individuals, who distinguished themselves so positively from the majority complicit in the regime’s violence, is the purpose of these lines. They are not written with the virtuosity of a professional author but as a report of lived and deeply felt experiences.

If this contribution can provide inquisitive youth with an unvarnished view of a fragment of the Third Reich’s history and put the concept of “command necessity” into proper perspective, then it is worth having endured and witnessed these events.
Author

Until April 1934, I lived in Munich but was forced to leave for Strasbourg early on the morning of April 20, driven out by the SA. As a member of the Socialist Party, I was unable to return to Vienna, my hometown, as the political situation there made it inadvisable.

My stay in France lasted only until the end of 1934. I faced difficulties obtaining a work permit, and working illegally carried risks I was unwilling to take. Therefore, I sought to reach Czechoslovakia by avoiding German-controlled territory, as it was considered a model host country for socialist emigrants.

Even the journey, with all its challenges, would be worth recounting in detail. With very little travel money and struggling with language barriers, I traveled through Moravia. Human kindness and cruelty lay so close together that one could never be sure whether they faced a friend or an enemy. Uncertainty bred hesitation and distrust.

After nearly three months of wandering, I finally found work and shelter in February 1935 in Brno, the capital of Moravia. I lived there for over two years, modestly but relatively free of worry, despite my limited language skills.

The continued pressure of the National Socialists on Austria eventually loosened the persecution of socialists there. Hoping for better economic prospects, I moved to Vienna in January 1937. At the time, the two-state theory was widely believed, and who could have predicted the extent of Hitler’s ambitions?

The year 1937 passed quickly. I met and fell in love with my wife, marrying her on February 6, 1938. During our honeymoon, we were startled by news of Schuschnigg’s visit to Obersalzberg. The communiqué from this meeting made me sense what was to come, and I began planning another emigration.
However, the looming difficulties proved insurmountable. I faced the events that followed without the slightest resistance.

The German invasion of Austria on March 10, 1938, my arrest on March 22, 1938, and my deportation to the labor camp in Kaprun on November 10, 1938 (“Kristallnacht”), culminating in the first transport of Jews from Vienna to Poland on January 10, 1940—these are dates well-documented in literature and the press and need not be elaborated here. My personal experiences, however, are, to my knowledge, unknown to the public, and I wish to begin by recounting the story of the first transport of Viennese Jews to Opole, in Lublin County.

In the first week of January 1940, an order from the “Emigration Office” on Argentinierstraße arrived at our home. We were to report to the relocation camp at Sperlschule by January 12, bringing no more than 20 kilograms of luggage, including two blankets. Furthermore, we were instructed to lock our apartments and bring the keys with us.

We knew from the press about Hitler’s order to make Vienna “Jewfree” as soon as possible. Rumors spread rapidly, claiming that Jews were now being gathered into designated ghettos. The daily harassment—both small and large—the hostility of much of the population, incited by vicious propaganda, and the open display of their disdain were all part of a calculated effort to make this “relocation” seem more acceptable to us. But who among us had the imagination to foresee what was truly coming? We knew of the horrific conditions in the concentration camps, of the sadistic torment inflicted by individual guards, and even of the cruelty of mobs during so-called “people’s uprisings,” which we had experienced firsthand.

Yet, we knew nothing of the perfectly orchestrated machinery of murder—of gas chambers, of mass executions! Who could have believed that the nation of poets and thinkers, the great German cultural nation, was capable of such things? During World War I, reports of German atrocities spread through the world press, but most Central Europeans dismissed them as mere propaganda. We ignored every warning from well-meaning neutrals, even from emigrants who had found refuge abroad, simply because we could not believe such things were possible. Even when faced with undeniable evidence, we downplayed events as wartime excesses. After all, we considered ourselves German citizens, not enemy aliens. We spoke the same language, had the same education, and saw ourselves as part of the same culture.

Despite the discrimination we faced because of our ancestry, we were a peaceful group who wanted nothing more than to be left alone. That is why we offered no resistance. The accusations from younger generations of Israeli Jews, who ask why we did not fight back, are misplaced. Most of the youth who might have resisted had already emigrated.
There were no signs of resistance among us, nothing that would have required harsh suppression. Yet, the authorities acted as though they feared us. We could not understand any of it.

I leave it to historians to explain the extent to which internal political difficulties or other factors led to these events. We, the victims, followed orders almost without protest, shaken by the indifference of other nations. Nowhere was there any willingness to accept the unfortunate refugees; everywhere, bureaucratic hurdles and insurmountable obstacles blocked the way. Humanity seemed to have vanished. Only those with financial means had any chance of emigrating. Large and small countries alike failed us. If one compares this with how Austria—still under Four-Power occupation—opened its borders after the Hungarian uprising of 1956, without asking whether the refugees had money or possessions, the contrast is staggering. A wave of global solidarity and aid followed in that case.

But from 1938 to 1945, there was a collapse of humanity on an unimaginable scale. That is the true reason we, leaderless and desperate, walked toward our doom. We felt betrayed and abandoned by the entire world.

At the school on Große Sperlgasse (the counterpart to the school on Castellezgasse), those who had been summoned gathered—mostly elderly couples carrying only their most essential belongings, as only kilograms of luggage was permitted. However, very few adhered to this limit. How could they? A bit of bedding and clothing, some cooking utensils, cherished personal items, and the required two blankets—20 kilograms were simply not enough. Their luggage resembled that of Russian Jewish petty bourgeoisie fleeing pogroms in earlier times. It was a sorrowful sight.

The school was under the control of the infamous SS Untersturmführer Brunner II. He was assisted by a Jewish orderly service, tasked with rounding up those who were late and assigning arrivals to empty classrooms. For more than three days, we had to endure the barren rooms as best we could. We slept on the floor, using one blanket as a mattress, our backpacks as pillows, and the second blanket for warmth. A kind of stew was distributed from a communal kitchen for lunch and dinner, along with bread from a school storeroom.

The heartbreak and despair that unfolded during those days are beyond description. I can only offer glimpses to help convey the reality. Elderly, frail couples leaned on one another for support. Individuals arrived alone. Families with children—mostly adults over fifty—gathered in silence. Young people were few. Fear was etched into every face. Everyone looked like hunted prey. Every conversation revolved around personal tragedies—each story a novel of human suffering. It was overwhelming to witness so much misery, fear, and helplessness crammed into such a confined space. The air was thick with sorrow, a crushing sense of despair that stripped away all will to resist.

As many as forty people were packed into a single classroom. Living in such close quarters, with people of different backgrounds and temperaments, bred hysteria. Petty matters that would have been ignored in normal times erupted into heated arguments, leading to hostility and incomprehensible hatred—even though we were all fellow sufferers, bound by the same fate.

For me, these events had an unexpected effect. Always trying to mediate in disputes, I found myself shaken out of my own despair and apathy. It was as if I had been given a stimulant, jolting me into action.

I forgot my own fate and began helping others. Wherever there was need, I stepped in. I assisted the elderly in setting up their sleeping areas, tidied during the day, made room for new arrivals, comforted the desperate, and handed out medicine to the sick. My efforts were noticed, and I was soon approached by Commander Brunner with a question that troubled me deeply—whether I wanted to stay behind until all deportations from Vienna were completed. Given my attachment to the city and the uncertainty of what lay ahead, it was a tempting offer. But witnessing the daily horrors of our situation, I rejected it. I longed for the “relocation” to its supposed destination—a place in occupied Poland that promised work and rest.

On the evening of January 15, 1941, we were called up one by one. Beforehand, we were ordered to surrender all valuables, especially money. Hiding anything, we were warned, would have “terrible consequences,” even the death penalty. We had to sign a document—one we were not even allowed to read. A brief, discreet glance revealed that we were signing over all our possessions, including our abandoned homes, to the German Reich. It was outright theft. Money and jewelry were tossed into a drawer without receipts, and we were each given twenty Polish zlotys as “travel money.”
This moment shattered us. We stared at each other in silent shock, unable to speak.

At four in the morning, we were herded into waiting trucks and driven to the Aspang Railway Station. There, we joined another group from the Castellezgasse school and were loaded into old, decommissioned passenger train cars. The Jewish Community provided us with food parcels, and then the train departed. Almost everyone wept. Yet, in our compartment, an air of forced optimism took hold. My wife and I were traveling with the Birnbach family—he was a pharmacist from Praterstraße, with two daughters aged fifteen and sixteen. Slowly, the mood shifted toward hopeful denial. I even exclaimed as we passed the Ferris wheel: “We’ll see each other again, even if it takes a while!”
The journey was slow, with few stops, moving at the pace of a freight train.

At Oderberg, during a longer stop, I went to fetch water. Upon my return, the Birnbach daughters greeted me with laughter. A policeman nearby muttered: “You’ll lose that laughter soon enough!” Did he already know our fate?

After more than forty hours, we arrived late at night in Nalęczów, where we were to transfer to another train bound for Opole. As we disembarked, German police and local collaborators beat us with whips. The sick and elderly, burdened by their luggage, fled from the blows in terror.
Even in the bitter cold, we were grateful to survive the journey. But this was only the beginning.

As we disembarked from the freight cars in Opole, we were once again met with shouting, cursing, and scolding from the gendarmes standing on the platform. Whips cracked, though less on the bodies of those getting out and more in the air, meant to hasten our exit from the station.

On the platform stood Jewish residents of Opole, who offered their help, especially assisting the elderly and those with mobility issues. They also showed us the way to the small town of Opole, about two kilometers from the station. The entire transport—consisting of approximately 1,030 men, women, and children—marched at midnight, carrying our luggage and what remained of the provisions we had received in Vienna. We were accompanied by young local Jews to a prepared overnight shelter, which was the town’s synagogue, cleared for this purpose. Though it was January, the winter in Opole was unusually mild, with temperatures just above freezing, so at least we were not cold.

Upon entering the synagogue, we found ourselves in a state of complete chaos. Everyone stood bewildered, unable to grasp the new reality. The initial intimidation of being greeted with dog whips contrasted sharply with the heartfelt welcome from the local Jews, yet the sight of our primitive lodgings was disheartening. It was a terrible dissonance—the vast prayer hall, now a desecrated sanctuary, stripped bare, with straw covering the floor. This was to serve as our living and

blocks.caption: Ruth and Rafuel Marcovici in Paris,

blocks.text: sleeping space for who knows how long. Scattered luggage, the suffocating crowd, frightened elderly people, crying children, a jumble of voices—an almost unfathomable confusion.

Yet, instinctively, the local Jews’ compassion was awakened. A wave of sympathy and willingness to help flooded us. Each local took in people as guests in their homes without expecting any compensation. This was no easy decision, as the Jewish community of Opole—about 3,500 people—had already been dispossessed of their homes by order of the district governor, none other than the infamous Viennese bookseller Amon Göth. They had been forced into a fenced-off ghetto, cut off from the Polish population. Despite their own suffering, they showed an incredible willingness to help complete strangers, even though we did not speak their language. This act of kindness deserves to be remembered by future generations.

A kind young couple approached my wife and invited her to come with them. Despite their warm words, we hesitated at first, especially because my wife was so exhausted from the events that she only wanted to lie down and rest. It took a firm and encouraging speech from the young man before we picked up our belongings again and followed them, feeling lost and worried—what would the future bring?

After about ten minutes of walking, we arrived at a house that looked quite plain from the outside but was spotlessly clean inside. It consisted of a kitchen, a small hallway, a living room, and two bedrooms. This was the home of the pharmacist Geszychter, who lived there with his wife, two daughters, and their husbands.

I will always remember the warm welcome we received, which will remain in my memory for the rest of my life. The heads of the household greeted us as if we were long-lost siblings visiting from afar. They welcomed us with heartfelt warmth, soothing words of sympathy, and a generous meal. They served us food we had long been deprived of—it was like a fairy tale. Soon, our initial shyness faded, and despite the late hour, we began sharing our stories with our eager hosts. We spoke about our experiences, believing them to be the worst possible hardships, but when we heard the stories from Opole, we realized the extent of suffering endured under Nazi rule. Fear crept in—what would the future hold for us? Everything was uncertain. A mountain of questions, the weight of worries, and unspoken fears cast a shadow over what would have otherwise been pleasant hours.

Eventually, exhaustion took over. My wife and I lay down on makeshift beds in a small room. At first, we fell into a deep sleep, but soon we tossed and turned, haunted by heavy dreams, until we awoke late in the morning.

I still vividly remember the figure of old Mr. Geszychter—tall, intelligent, with sharp eyes hidden behind modern glasses, always twinkling mischievously at visitors. He had an imposing presence, always ready with a joke, always wearing an ironic smile, downplaying unpleasant events and embracing life with optimism. His kind and understanding nature earned my deepest admiration. I deeply regret that fate never allowed me to meet him again after those difficult times. However, in my heart, I have built a monument to this wonderful man, which I will always look up to.

His wife, a doctor, was a perfect counterpart to him. Despite her somewhat heavyset figure, she had an innate charm. Her friendly, always playfully twinkling eyes and dimples made her an endearing sight. The way she offered food and encouraged guests to eat was so delightful that one simply could not refuse. Everything she did came from a place of genuine warmth and human kindness. She came from a wealthy family of jewelers in Tsarist Russia—the Szlezzynger family from St. Petersburg. Her entire demeanor, every gesture, spoke of her refined upbringing. Her empathy and understanding revealed her medical background. Like her husband, I hold her in the highest regard and gratitude.

Throughout my life, I have experienced much ingratitude, even from people to whom I had given much—even their lives. I endured cruelty and suffered under the selfishness of others, almost to the point of losing faith in humanity. But the memory of the wonderful Geszychter couple kept my belief in the goodness of people alive.

Their well-mannered daughters and their husbands stood out pleasantly among the Jewish residents of Opole. Always polite and helpful, they did everything they could to assist us. Communication was possible because I spoke some Yiddish, though the common language in Congress Poland was Polish and Russian.

To illustrate the depth of hospitality we received, I must mention one episode. The Geszychter family was highly secular, considering Jewish religious laws outdated and irrelevant. The commandments of Moses, in the eyes of Poland’s Jewish intelligentsia, were seen as an unnecessary burden, merely an excuse for the cruel persecution they faced.

Yet, knowing that I adhered to a more traditional faith, they made an exception. Though they had never held a Passover Seder before, they followed my instructions to prepare a fully ritual Seder for both nights, and I was asked to lead it. This might sound simple, but considering the preparations involved, it was extraordinary. For the first time, they koshered their dishes, acquired kosher meat (previously unknown in their household), and procured matzah and kosher wine. All this effort—for an unwilling guest, forcibly sent by the Nazis into an unfamiliar environment.

The atmosphere of those two evenings is difficult to describe. Everyone listened intently as I recited the Haggadah, observing the rituals with deep reverence. It was touching to see young and old alike absorbing the story of the Exodus as if it were a revelation.
It felt like a reflection of our own reality—as if the oppressed and persecuted Jews under the Nazi yoke were once again hoping for a leader to guide them from suffering and disgrace into freedom.

At first, we were accommodated in an abandoned house—on corridors, in the attic, and on the stairwell. The Judenrat in Irena, a nearby suburb, was notified. In the late afternoon, we received our first meal of this dreadful day: bread, jam, and tea. The floor was our only bedding.

We were grateful that outside, it was a wonderful spring day with warm sunshine—a stark contrast to our state of mind. We looked ahead to the next morning with anxiety. It was not hard to imagine the fear of those who had been left behind. I believe that none of us slept that night—not just because of the discomfort.

After the difficult night, we had a roll call in the morning. We were taken outside the airbase to a newly built, spacious wooden barrack. Inside, we found wooden bunks with thickly stuffed straw mattresses and two wool blankets each. This was to be our new lodging. After everyone had chosen a sleeping spot, we were led to the airbase for work.

Our guards were soldiers—evidently, frontline-ineligible troops—who reassured us and eased our initial terror. We were assigned to road construction and the building of embankments to shield aircraft from enemy air attacks since there were too few hangars at the airfield. The runway had been significantly expanded to accommodate numerous combat aircraft.

A fortunate coincidence occurred after just over eight days, shaping my fate, as well as that of my family and nearly 900 Jews, until our deportation on July 23, 1944. When I reflect on this moment now, nearly 26 years later, I must humbly acknowledge the workings of a higher power.

I was working at a concrete mixer. As I looked up from my task—being accustomed to staying alert from my previous work at the Tauern Power Plant—I noticed an officer observing us. Judging by his sleeve insignia, he was a Luftwaffe captain. At first, I couldn’t believe my eyes and thought my imagination was deceiving me. But the more I looked, the more certain I became.

It was a war comrade from the Austrian Infantry Regiment No. 24, whom I had carried on my back, severely wounded, through enemy fire from a hilltop on the Doberdò plateau to the medical post in August 1918. Normally, I was never plagued by feelings of inferiority, but at that moment, I hesitated to reveal myself. I had no idea how he would react. We had already endured bitter experiences.
Nevertheless, I decided to step forward despite my dirt-covered clothing and disheveled appearance.

At first, he was indignant at the audacity of a Jew, marked by a white armband called a “Lente,” addressing him. But as soon as I mentioned my name, my regiment, and the incident at Doberdò in a concise and military manner, he became approachable.

To the astonishment of my fellow workers and the guards, he embraced me and asked how I had ended up there. After I gave him a brief explanation, he went to the commanding officer and ordered that I be cleaned up and prepared for collection by a patrol the next morning.

As soon as he left, my comrades bombarded me with questions, wanting to know what had happened. I could only recount the past events, but I had no idea what lay ahead. That night, I was once again unable to sleep, my thoughts racing. I imagined the most unlikely possibilities—including returning with my family to Vienna. The initial belief that relocation to Poland meant a reprieve for us had turned out to be a terrible illusion. I feared the worst, but no one could have foreseen the horrors that would actually unfold.

The next morning at five o’clock, I was exceptionally allowed to leave the barracks for proper cleaning. At the well, I scrubbed myself thoroughly. Using the meager supplies available, I cleaned my shoes and clothes as best as I could, so that I looked somewhat presentable.

At 8:30 AM, a military policeman from the airbase escorted me to the airfield commandant’s office. I had to wait in the antechamber of Lieutenant Colonel Hönig, constantly scrutinized by passing officers and soldiers.
At exactly 9 AM, my former war comrade, Captain Eduard Bromofsky, entered the room. After some brief instructions, during which he addressed me informally, I was led into the inner office.
After a military-style report, I was introduced with the following words:
“Colonel, this is the war comrade I told you about, the one who saved my life. I would be very grateful if you could somehow help him, even though he has the misfortune of being a Jew.”

The lieutenant colonel scrutinized me for a long time. My upright posture and brief, precise answers seemed to make an impression. He picked up the phone and contacted the chief quartermaster, Wolfram, who was in charge of the Jewish labor camp.

The colonel informed him that he was assigning him an “exceptional Jew” and would discuss the details in person. I was instructed to report to Wolfram immediately, who would handle everything else. As I was dismissed, he reassured me:
“You don’t need to be afraid. In the Wehrmacht, especially among the airmen, you are in good hands.”
After expressing my gratitude, I left the office and waited outside for the captain to tell me what would happen next.
A few minutes later, Bromofsky returned and said:

“Hermann, you are really lucky! The lieutenant colonel took a liking to you and ordered the quartermaster to take care of you and make your situation easier. I also discussed your future with him. He explicitly told me: If Jews have any chance of survival in Poland, it is only in a labor camp—especially one run by the Wehrmacht. All existing ghettos are temporary; only those fit for work have a chance to survive. Everything else is doomed to destruction. If you want your wife and family to live, get them out of Opole and bring them here. I will help you as much as I can.”
I thanked him warmly and went to Wolfram’s office to learn more.

When I reflect on these events, it is clear that by 1940, higher command knew exactly what terrible fate awaited both deported and native Jews. That is why I always doubt when older people claim that only a few knew about the horrors, violence, and atrocities, because a “wall of silence” surrounded everything.

It is impossible for me to believe that so many accomplices remained silent. It seems more likely that it was a case of willful ignorance—a refusal to know, to silence a guilty conscience. That, I think, best characterizes this era. Millions of soldiers, civilians—including many opponents of the regime—and hundreds of thousands of forced laborers from occupied territories all supposedly remained silent or encountered disbelief? No rational person can accept that.
Even today, people have a tendency to trivialize things.

Through the quartermaster’s intervention, I was assigned to the Luftwaffe’s construction department under Engineer Figura. He appointed me as the group leader of about 100 Jews working in road construction for the Viennese company Autheried.

I was responsible for payroll, food rations, and even procuring essential medicines. This work gave me great satisfaction, as I was able to help those who were suffering in ways I had never imagined possible.
My position at Autheried not only benefited me—it later allowed me, with the support of the airbase commandant, to bring my wife from Opole to me.
Anyone who knew the conditions in occupied Poland can understand what a lifeline this was.

I carried the child in my arms, expecting that a patrol might stop me or even shoot at me without warning. All of this was within the realm of possibility, and yet I walked without fear because I felt deep inside that everything had to turn out well. Not just this time, but in all other precarious situations, I had this good, optimistic feeling. I don’t know what to attribute this to; in any case, I believed I was safe and would survive all danger unscathed.

The joy of my relatives was unimaginable when I arrived with Ruthi. They had been very worried about us, fearing the worst, and now they saw us safe and healthy. It was one of the happiest days of that terrible time.

I still remember the first deportation very well. It was in April 1941. First, the town of Ryki, about six kilometers away, was targeted. As I had probably already mentioned, I sat safely in the construction barrack of the “Auteried” and watched from the office window as the unfortunate people marched past. They were driven by the green police and the SS. The old and the young, struggling under the weight of their bundles, which likely contained only hastily gathered belongings—clothes, linens, or similar necessities. It was an unimaginable scene of horror: masses of people gasping under their burdens, whips cracking on those who stumbled, hesitated, or fell. Cracking gunshots, and at the end of the procession, Panjewagen (horse-drawn carts) full of corpses, bore witness to the terrible fate of those who had collapsed from exhaustion. For the first time, I saw the true face of the Nazi terror regime. For the first time, I witnessed such unspeakable brutality, the kind I could never have imagined. I would witness many more cruelties later, but that march of horror surpassed everything my mind could comprehend.

I had heard of atrocities—but to see organized mass murder, not even cloaked in a political pretense, with my own eyes was something else entirely. I am grateful that it was also the only time I saw it firsthand.

At this point, I feel compelled to address those who argue that enough has been written about these events, that we should finally put this unpleasant past behind us. I must insist that they are gravely mistaken. Only constant remembrance can prevent the regrettably large number of indifferent people from being drawn into the wake of those who still seek to justify that murderous regime. Those who did not experience it, who only know of these horrors secondhand, will struggle to understand that such things could have happened. No human imagination can fully grasp the reality of those events. The ingenuity of the sadists of the “Third Reich” was so vast that it surpassed even the most vivid imagination. The slogan “Never forget!” must be reinforced by continuous reminders so that it becomes “Never again!”

The crimes committed were so outrageous that no perpetrator should ever be granted a statute of limitations or clemency. Any attempt to whitewash or forget serves only to make this shameful chapter of German history socially acceptable again—as we can see on the current political stage. I am the staunchest opponent of collective guilt for an entire people, but for the crimes of the rulers of that so-called “Thousand-Year Reich,” there can be no pardon. In the name of the millions of tortured people across Europe and those displaced by the consequences of the lost war, I appeal to responsible politicians: avoid any form of assistance or connection, no matter how opportune, with those who still pull the strings of a resurgent movement that has already brought so much suffering to humanity. This general detour from my personal experiences seems necessary to me because it provides context for the following lines.

Some time before the German invasion of Russia, the construction barracks on the Vistula River were completed, surrounded by a wire fence, and occupied by Jewish and Polish forced laborers. The diagram on the following page illustrates the situation. I became the administrative clerk of the camp, which was under the supervision of a Luftwaffe non-commissioned officer. To assist me in the office, we brought in fellow Viennese Jews due to their proficiency in German. For medical care, we brought Dr. Wasservogel from Vienna and the dentist Zissu from Opole as a medic and for dental care.

The area where the camp was located was part of the Vistula floodplain, making it damp and swampy. Fifty meters away stood the Luftwaffe construction office, making it easy to reach our supposed protectors. Diagonally across from us was the air force fuel depot.

The first camp leader was Sergeant Kattengel from Magdeburg, who held office until March 1943. His small rank did not stop him from acting like a grand mogul. The authority over several hundred Jews intoxicated him with power. It required a great deal of diplomacy, combined with leveraging my good relations with the construction management and the commandant’s office, to keep him somewhat in check. I can still see him before me, accompanied by a massive black wolfhound, a dog whip in his hand, strutting through the camp like a petty tyrant.

We received our wages from the Luftwaffe construction office and were provided with rations calculated at 1,700 calories. We had our own camp administration and even ten Jewish camp policemen. We formed a self-contained community, while the Luftwaffe only provided our guards and otherwise left us to our own devices.

We collected our food rations weekly, and when we noticed that Kattengel was siphoning off supplies for himself, I managed—through tact and intervention with the construction office—to ensure that the food was stored in our own camp warehouse. Kattengel was left only with the responsibility of managing food supplies for the Poles.

Shortly before the war against Russia, typhus broke out in the camp, probably brought in by Warsaw Jews who had recently arrived. The camp was placed under quarantine. Only Dr. Wasservogel and I, since

blocks.caption: Oberrabbiner Paul Chaim Eisenberg, Ruth Marcovici ca. 1983

blocks.text: I lived in town, were allowed to come and go, maintaining our connection to the outside world. We had been vaccinated against typhus in time and were very careful. Nevertheless, Dr. Wasservogel became infected and died in a Lublin hospital. We mourned his death deeply, and soon after, the dentist Zissu also passed away from a heart attack. For some time, we were left without a doctor in the camp until, in October 1942, after the last deportation from Irena, Dr. Kestenbaum from Ryki, Dr. Rosenbluth from Tarnów, and the medic Frym joined us. Additionally, veterinarian Dr. Fries arrived at the camp. We established a medical station and a kind of infirmary, always stocked with enough medications, which we received partly from the Jewish Aid Office in Kraków, partly from the commandant’s office from Wehrmacht supplies, and also from purchases at the pharmacy in Irena.

Given what I learned after the war, I can say with pride that our camp was perhaps the only one for Jews that remained so well-equipped until our evacuation to Częstochowa on June 23, 1944. Even after the SS took over the camp in late 1943, we managed to supplement the reduced rations with vegetables from Naleczów and our own garden, so that when our inmates arrived in Częstochowa, they were stared at like miraculous beings from another world.

Without boasting, I can claim much of what was achieved was due to my ever-watchful mind, my initiative in seizing every opportunity, and my unyielding activity in every situation. That such conditions existed in the years of chaos, the looming military defeat, and the blind, raging fury of the SS seems unbelievable, but it is the truth.

At that time, influential members of the Jewish Council wanted to appoint me as its president due to my German language skills and good relationships with the Germans. Although this position came with advantages, I declined because collaboration with the green police, the SS, and German administration seemed too dubious. Somehow, I still had more trust in the Wehrmacht.

The outbreak of war with Russia also led to a tighter organization of the camp. Soldiers were stationed outside as guards, while inside, control numbers were issued, and the camp population was meticulously checked on a weekly basis. When I received word from the commandant’s office that Irena was to be completely evacuated in the autumn of 1942, I dissolved my household in the ghetto and brought my entire family into the camp. This presented me with a problem that caused me much anxiety. According to camp regulations, only those fit for work—meaning no children—could be admitted. Fortunately, female labor was employed at Horst, in agriculture, and in the company. The large kitchen and the tailoring workshop also employed women. This gave me enough opportunities to place female family members, but there was no chance for children, especially toddlers like Ruthi, to be admitted.

With this concern, I turned once again to Wolfram, who had already proven to be a guardian angel once before. Unfortunately, Captain Bromofsky had since been transferred to the front, and despite diligent inquiries, I had heard nothing more of him. But a miracle happened. Wolfram contacted Lieutenant Colonel Hönig, a very humane officer. He explained that keeping families together would be beneficial for morale and willingness to work. As a result, I was granted permission to register all children as agricultural assistants, assign them control numbers, and thus officially count them as camp inmates. One cannot imagine my joy when I returned from the commandant’s office and was able to share this wonderful news with the anxiously waiting parents.

Enough years have passed to have some distance from these events. When I consider the well-known realities of the East, it seems incredible that something like this could have happened. Certainly, a great deal of pragmatic calculation played a role in carrying out this humane act—the desire to maintain our workforce and a general indifference to what happened within the camp. But the very fact that the commandant’s office knew everything and tolerated it clearly shows that it was possible for superiors—even in a small labor camp—to save lives. Our guards did not think much about the presence of children, especially since they saw them gathering potatoes or performing other tasks and thus classified them as workers.

Following this, I obtained permission to establish a barrack specifically for families. Two women acted as kindergarten teachers, allowing parents to go to work with peace of mind. The children were taught reading and writing, and they were even taught various games. The camp became a community, almost as if in freedom. A “Chevra Kadisha” (Sacred Brotherhood) was established to conduct burials, and the dead were buried in proper shrouds. Religious services were held on holidays. During Passover, matzot was baked in the camp, and Orthodox Jews were reportedly kept back in the camp on the Sabbath under the pretense of being assigned cleaning duties to prevent them from working on the holy day. It all sounds unbelievable, but it really happened. The camp commandant was interested in preserving our labor force but otherwise left us in peace.

By September 1942, the camp population stood at 542 Jews, but after the final evacuation in Irena, it grew to 970. The group was a diverse mix, even in terms of nationality. The majority were Polish, followed by Slovaks, Hungarians, Austrians, and Germans from the old Reich.

The camp was also diverse in character and social background. Aside from the very small number of intellectuals, there were many craftsmen, small merchants, and also many unskilled workers and illiterates. Many, driven by selfishness and the instinct for self-preservation, sought only personal advantages, regardless of the community. Maintaining a structured camp discipline for the benefit of all was perhaps the hardest challenge. The well-intentioned were in the minority, while the undisciplined were capable of reckless actions. Additionally, almost daily, there was the nearly impossible task of maintaining an order acceptable to both the construction management and the commandant’s office. It required every ounce of concentration to stay focused on work, and I wonder today how I managed it. I believe my unyielding will to survive, my concern for my family, and my unbreakable optimism in even the most dangerous situations were the driving forces behind my success.

There were indeed many dangerous—life-threatening—situations. I will try to describe a few events that illustrate the circumstances. But first, I want to discuss the difficulties of my role as the camp’s administrative officer. Many envied me. A foreigner was in charge—one who refused to engage in corruption, who was immune to bribes, and who insisted on Western-style order and discipline, almost like the Germans? This was unheard of for the Poles, and I made enemies even among my closest colleagues, who tried with all means—fair or foul—to bring me down. I learned of reports against me to the commandant’s office, the construction management, the gendarmerie, and even the German camp leader and German supervisors of the labor groups. Only the special respect I had earned over four years through my steadfast conduct ensured that all such attempts failed. I saw many of these denunciations—they were shown to me—but I took no action, even though I certainly had the means to seek retribution. I knew that primitive revenge would only make matters worse, that I could expect even more hostility from the punished person’s relatives, and that this would endanger not only me but many others as well. In this case, caution was the better part of valor, and time ultimately proved me right.

September 22 was the day of the final deportation from Irena. Early in the morning, SS officer Lieutenant Philippi visited the camp leader, Kattengel, who soon called for me. I was asked how many people were currently in the camp. I reported the number as 543.
I was then given the following categorical order with particular emphasis:

“Today, there is a deportation in Irena. No one may leave the camp. No one may go to work at Horst. Tomorrow, I will come, and if I find even a single Jew from Irena in the camp, I will ensure that the SS from Lublin liquidates the entire camp! The non-commissioned officer and you bear full responsibility for the strict enforcement of this order.”

The news was devastating. I knew that many relatives of my fellow prisoners were still in Irena. A deportation would bring despair and severely weaken the discipline we had worked so hard to maintain, putting us all in danger. What could I do under a camp leader who was all too eager to carry out orders? I dared not make such a serious decision alone. So, I decided to consult a group of respected older men whom I highly valued—Eckhaiser, Bronspiegel, Schulmann, Dr. Kestenbaum, and Dr. Fries. Together, we decided to warn the Jews of Irena and to try to obtain a blank work assignment from the German head of the agricultural department, who was open to gifts, so that we could officially register as many Irena Jews as possible as farm workers. This would provide cover against Lieutenant Philippi. It was a well-thought-out plan—but I was the one tasked with executing it. My objections were dismissed with the phrase, “God will help.”

I set out. The “Council of Elders” secretly alerted the Jews of Irena, and as the work groups returned, over four hundred people flooded into the camp—including the nearly 80-year-old Rosenzweig couple and many children whom I was to list as workers.

The plan with the agricultural supervisor worked perfectly. A pair of new boots, a kilo of coffee, and two bottles of vodka were the “open sesame.” To avoid the appearance of a bribe, I paid the official fraction of the black-market price. At the construction office, I legalized the agricultural assignment, and in record time, I arranged the necessary paperwork. Control numbers and food stamps were issued, Kattengel was persuaded, and we anxiously awaited what would come next.
Against all expectations, Lieutenant Philippi forgot to visit, and our fear of a thorough inspection was alleviated.

Gendarmerie Master Petersen arrived at the camp and summoned Kattengel, who immediately granted him unrestricted entry, despite the strict directive from the construction management stating that entry into the Jewish camp was only permitted to outsiders with their approval. I did not dare to deny Petersen entry, even though I suspected he would only cause trouble. Petersen asked for the young man, Puterflam. When I inquired about the matter, he explained that he was investigating a report by the foreman Rudolph from the Berlin construction company Schulz, which alleged that Puterflam had repeatedly left his workplace without reason and had not shown up that day.

“I want to have a look at the boy,” Petersen declared. Since it was nearly the end of the workday and the first groups were already marching through the gate, I had no choice but to excuse Puterflam by claiming he was ill. Puterflam was indeed an extremely frail eighteenyear-old, and it was easy to believe that he was physically incapable of enduring the grueling labor of track construction. Unfortunately, the labor assignments at Schulz were not under our control but were overseen by the Irena Labor Office. The Schulz company merely had the right to house a group of forced laborers with us if they could not be accommodated in the company’s own quarters. This was arranged contractually because Schulz was also carrying out road construction projects for Horst.

I was powerless to help Puterflam and had to watch helplessly as events unfolded. At Petersen’s order, Kattengel called for a roll call. All camp inmates had to assemble, and in front of everyone, Petersen shouted at Puterflam:
“Why are you sabotaging, you Jewish swine? You know how crucial it is to complete the tracks for the war effort! You know that every worker is needed!”

Puterflam timidly protested that he was ill, to which Petersen responded with a powerful strike from Kattengel’s dog whip. Screaming in pain, Puterflam collapsed onto a bench behind him, and mercilessly, a hail of blows rained down on his writhing body. Puterflam staggered and fell to the ground. In an instant, his tormentor drew his pistol and shot the boy in the back of the head. A brief twitch, then his body lay still.

Then came the repulsive scene that I can only recall with the greatest disgust. Petersen pressed his boot against the dead boy’s throat, causing his pain-distorted mouth to open and his bloody tongue to protrude.

A sigh of relief escaped Petersen’s chest, and with a deep exhale, he concluded his crime—a crime that bore witness to the full perversion of this so-called “superhuman.” Petersen undoubtedly had enough intelligence to understand that his act was cruel, inhumane, and sadistic. It could not be excused as mere brute force or thoughtless cruelty. Rather, it was a deliberately cultivated perversion, fueled by the incessantly propagated hatred toward all people who were not privileged to belong to the so-called master race.

The second incident also involved workers from the Schulz company. Kattengel received orders from foreman Rudolph (or perhaps even from the construction manager, Gailhorn?) to detain two specifically named workers in the camp. Lacking a designated holding cell, he devised the idea of enclosing a three-square-meter area with barbed wire at the camp’s perimeter and imprisoning the men there. They spent a night within the confined space, and the next day, Rudolph arrived with the gendarme Edek. They had Kattengel hand over the detainees. Behind the camp, out of our view due to the non-commissioned officers’ barracks, we heard two short gunshots. When we reached the camp gate, we were ordered to bury the dead.

This disgraceful act strengthened my resolve to have Kattengel replaced. But that was easier said than done. Openly speaking against him was not only dangerous for me but for the entire camp. To make matters worse, Kattengel had developed an intimate relationship with a young Jewish girl, whose mother and two brothers were also in the camp. She did not hesitate to exploit this connection to gain privileges—not just for herself but also for other inmates who paid for her intervention. In short, widespread corruption of the most troubling kind began to emerge, as it became known throughout the camp and could endanger everyone. Furthermore, other young girls, envious of her advantage, tried to do the same. Preventing such scandals required great tact, diplomacy, and self-control. After all, German soldiers were strictly forbidden from having any contact with Jewish women, and the consequences of such affairs could be disastrous for the entire camp.

I often had to stand guard at night to disrupt these flirtations and clandestine encounters. It is understandable that Kattengel held little sympathy for me because of this and eagerly lent his ear to inmates who harbored ill will against me, making my life more difficult. When I consider how precarious our situation was, it is still a mystery to me how these affairs remained hidden. Even a single one of these incidents coming to light would have been enough for the SS in Lublin, to whom we were officially subordinate, to liquidate us.

Another event put us on the brink of catastrophe. Those who know how ruthlessly the SS waged war against Polish partisans will understand my terror when, during the brutal winter nights of 1943, ten Jewish partisans arrived at the camp, seeking refuge.

We had men, women, and children in the camp, ranging in age from four to seventy-five. Accepting responsibility for the inevitable massacre that would follow if the presence of partisans were discovered was unthinkable. So, I decided to consult the trusted “Council of Elders” before making a decision. Our discussion took place with extreme caution, late at night when most were asleep. In the end, we resolved to take the partisans in, as we were faced with a choice between the SS’s vengeance and that of the partisans. Our sympathies naturally lay with the partisans, yet the decision was an enormous gamble.

However, accepting the partisans did not lead to disaster—quite the opposite. When a damaged, burning aircraft crashed nearby, two partisans working on the airfield saved the pilot, a Knight’s Cross recipient, and his co-pilot. They pulled the unconscious men from the cockpit. The event caused a sensation at the airfield, and our camp’s standing improved in the eyes of all military authorities. A few months later, the two rescued pilots arrived at our camp on horseback to personally express their gratitude, commend the rescuers, and promise to advocate for the camp. We knew then that our decision had been worth it.

In March 1943, when Kattengel was replaced by Staff Sergeant Dusy, I and other responsible comrades breathed a sigh of relief. At first, we feared that Dusy, a long-serving non-commissioned officer, would be a harsh disciplinarian. Initially, it seemed so, but soon it became clear that he was approachable and open to persuasion. Most importantly, I was pleased that he did not adhere to the Nazi ideology of Jewish “subhumanity.” He often spoke to me about his positive experiences with Jews in his hometown of Königsberg.

I never felt like a suppressed prisoner—I maintained my self-respect and dignity. Knowing that Dusy recognized and respected this was a great relief. Over time, we became better acquainted, and his initial aloofness disappeared. Dusy strictly enforced the rule that no outsiders could enter the camp without authorization, preventing SS members and gendarmerie from intruding as frequently.

His tenure lasted a little over a year. During that time, we had ample opportunity to observe him. His attitude toward us was more positive than negative. Though no person is entirely good, Dusy made an effort to ease our suffering. Given all the terrible experiences we had with Germans, he was a rare exception.

Braun had a smug manner, always asking questions, pointing out concerns about our well-being, and particularly complaining about the presence of eggshells and chicken bones—after all, signs of good nutrition. We all in the camp struggled to appease him. Everyone feared him because he was not to be trusted.

Our hope for a cooperative relationship rested on the fact that he had a sweetheart. She would come over from outside on weekends and make requests, especially to my wife and me, which we tried to fulfill. By today’s standards, they were modest demands—such as a handbag, an umbrella, a scarf, a blouse, or coffee beans—but under the circumstances at the time, obtaining such things was very difficult. Eager to stay on Braun’s good side, we ensured that these wishes were always granted.

Apart from minor harassments, nothing extraordinary happened under his rule, except for one incident I must mention: In March 1944, I was summoned to the camp gate, where a car with SS officers was waiting outside. Braun was there as well. I reported to them and was ordered to assign—or name—forty tailors and seamstresses. They were to be taken to Stawy the next morning to sew uniforms for Wehrmacht auxiliaries, who were already fleeing in panic from the advancing Russians.

I myself was aware of the conditions in Stawy from information that had come to me. Executions and mistreatment of Jews were a daily occurrence, and in my view, transferring our people there meant a death sentence. I desperately thought about how I could prevent this disaster. I can only attribute it to a higher inspiration that I came up with the idea to tell the leading Hauptsturmführer that our people would be willing to take on this work additionally, in their free time, given the urgency, and that only the materials needed to be brought to the camp. I believe my proposal was well received also because, due to the unexpected influx of people in Stawy, there was a lack of space. In any case, the idea was approved, and we were able to keep our people in the camp. But the best part was that after I reported this incident to the command, the operation was refused. The delivered materials and supplies were sent back, and the whole thing fizzled out. Within the camp itself, this matter was quickly forgotten, as people generally paid more attention to their daily worries than to major events, even after significant occurrences.

This attitude is easily explained by the uncertainty of one’s own fate, without any possibility of influencing it. People lived only for the present and left the future to a fortunate turn of fate. Indifference and resignation were widespread.

The year 1944 was marked by events on the collapsing Eastern Front. The relentless retreat did not escape our notice, and we also knew about the successful Western offensive of the Allies. Our informants were Poles who worked at the airbase and apparently had connections with partisans who had radio devices and received news from abroad. Even from the German side, we received information about the situation.

One time, while I was on duty heading to the command, I was approached by a uniformed German soldier who called me by name and introduced himself as an architect from Vienna. In our conversation, he mentioned the collapse of the Eastern Front and predicted that the Red Army would soon arrive in Dęblin. He offered me the opportunity to go underground with him, as he belonged to a resistance organization. He said it was the only way to save myself. He knew that when the Germans withdrew from Poland, all Jews would be exterminated beforehand. The “scorched earth” order had already been issued. I told him that I could not abandon my family, and he regretfully took his leave.

However, I also suspected him somewhat of being an agent provocateur. Even if I had no family in the camp, I would not have accepted his proposal, out of loyalty to my comrades. I told the “Council of Elders” about the incident, and they were pleased that I was thinking so responsibly.

At the beginning of July, I was introduced by Braun to a German civilian named Bartenschlager, who questioned me about the inmates of our camp. I had no way of knowing who Bartenschlager was or what role he would still play. I knew nothing about the purpose of his visit and assumed he was a personal acquaintance of the non-commissioned officer. I answered his questions very cautiously and reservedly. He himself was surprised that Jews in Poland in 1944 still looked so well-dressed and well-fed. I explained this by saying that our diligence, discipline, and hard work had earned us recognition from the command. When he said “Goodbye” upon leaving, I had no idea how soon we would meet again. Shortly afterward, Braun was unexpectedly replaced. His successor was Sergeant Rademacher from Berlin, an older, long-serving non-commissioned officer. He negotiated with me calmly and composedly, always valuing my opinion. I suspect that the command had recommended me to him as trustworthy.

On July 14, 1944, I was summoned to the chief staff quartermaster, our highest camp authority, who informed me that due to the advancing front, our camp would be evacuated to Częstochowa, to the Warthewerk, a munitions factory. The camp would fall under factory security, not the SS, and he had already spoken with the factory security chief. He assured me that we would be treated humanely there, similarly to how we were in Dęblin.

My first concern was for the fate of the women and children, as I knew from Polish and German reports what the SS did to Jewish children. Wolfram gave me his officer’s word of honor that nothing would happen to our children. I do not know what made me doubt his word and what gave me the courage to ask him for a letter signed by Colonel Hönig, the airbase commander, to reassure our mothers and fathers. I am glad I had this idea because this letter would ultimately save 36 children. Surprisingly, Wolfram did not reject my request; on the contrary, he promised to have such a letter given to the transport leader.

At this meeting, our labor deployment leader, the German Pole Wiszniewski, was present. He was ordered to prepare the camp for transport in three groups. The dates were set: Group I on July 17, Group II on July 20, and the last group on July 23, 1944. Wolfram explicitly insisted that the children be included proportionally so they would not stand out in large numbers and risk being singled out. Wiszniewski promised to follow orders and also to ensure family unity.
With very mixed feelings, I left the command and returned to the camp, full of concern. Our situation in Dęblin had been bearable compared to other camps, but what awaited us in Częstochowa?

From the command to the camp was about 2,500 meters. I rode a bicycle. Usually, it took ten minutes, but this time, I took almost twice as long. Filled with worry, I pondered the consequences of our relocation. I knew the dangers we faced under German rule. We were at the mercy of an individual’s whims, and the coming days were uncertain. Everything depended on chance. What were our chances of survival? All we had was hope.

What I had not accounted for was the reaction of my fellow inmates. That evening, I gathered the entire workforce, except for the children, and informed them of my conversation with Wolfram, including my efforts to secure the safety of the children and the written assurance. At first, there was dead silence. Then, an uproar of unexpected proportions erupted. Hysterical screaming broke out, like during a shipwreck. The crowd was in turmoil—otherwise calm men shouted over each other, mothers shrieked about the impending doom of their children, and everyone talked about escaping the camp. No reasonable argument was heard. It was as if hell had been unleashed.

I realized that the crowd was unreliable and called the “Council of Elders” to the office, as I could not get them to listen to reason. We discussed how to manage the relocation with as little hardship as possible, especially ensuring families were not separated, as we did not fully trust the promise that the entire camp would be transferred together. It was a very long discussion, and we went to bed filled with worry. The next morning, red eyes showed that hardly anyone had slept.

In the days leading up to our relocation, I witnessed more human weaknesses, ingratitude, and shortcomings than in my entire life. An unparalleled selfishness was unleashed. Formerly friendly faces contorted into grimaces, calm voices became shrill, and sheer fear made me the perfect scapegoat.

As the first transport left on July 17, under the supervision of seemingly trustworthy militia soldiers, our spirits calmed slightly. Events at the front escalated, and on July 22, we received the order to prepare for departure. The sight of waiting freight cars led some to suggest mass escape, but with women, children, and elderly people, such an attempt would have been suicide.
On the morning of July 23, we were finally loaded onto the trains. As we departed, we heard the distant rumble of the advancing front.

Late in the evening of July 25, we arrived in Częstochowa. Rademacher called the Warthewerk, our future place of stay, and was informed that we would be picked up the next day. We spent the night restlessly in the wagons. In the morning, the detached freight cars were moved from the sidings to the factory premises.

The sight of the electrified factory fence, the high watchtowers, and the heavily armed factory guards at the gate gave us a foreboding of a grim fate. We could see little more through the narrow gaps in the doors. When the train stopped, the wagon doors were flung open from the outside with shouting, and we were met by a crowd of factory guards in green uniforms. Some aimed submachine guns at us, while others lashed out wildly with whips, driving us out of the wagons.

We had no illusions about arriving in good conditions, but none of us had expected it to be this bad. Despair overtook us. I was accused of lacking the courage to make a better decision in Dęblin, and some claimed they could already smell gas when they saw the tall factory chimneys spewing thick smoke. To make matters worse, our children were separated from us. We had to put down our luggage and assemble for roll call in the vast factory courtyard, separated into men and women.
Sergeant Rademacher and his accompanying soldiers stood indifferently to the side, as if they had nothing to do with us. Our hope of be-

blocks.caption: Klara und Herrmann Wenkart ca. 1957, Vienna

blocks.text: ing under the Wehrmacht’s protection was shattered. A deep sorrow overcame me, and I feared as never before for the fate of my comrades and, above all, for my family. Up until now, even in the most hopeless situations, I had always kept my head up, always found some glimmer of hope. But this time, I truly believed our end had come.

To the side, the heavily guarded children were crying and calling out for their parents. Being separated from Ruthi, my mother-in-law, and my wife—without knowing what would become of them or of myself—was a crushing blow. No wonder I doubted myself. Had I been right to trust Wolfram’s assurances? These were moments of utter despair.

For over half an hour, we stood in formation, surrounded by factory guards who watched us grimly and menacingly, their submachine guns at the ready. Their ominous looks were so oppressive that we could only stare in silence at the children standing to the side. The wait for the factory security chief lasted only half an hour, but under such psychological strain, it felt like an eternity. We were so consumed by fear that we forgot to express any pain or make a single sound.

Finally, the awaited man arrived, accompanied by his deputy, Keutsch, and some factory guards. Since he wore a dark uniform resembling that of the SS, I did not immediately recognize him. It was only when he called out loudly, “Where is Wenkart?” and ordered me to step forward that I realized it was Bartenschlager—the very same Bartenschlager who had interrogated me in the camp about the prisoners, assessing their suitability for labor. His personal address snapped me out of my lethargy, and I regained some courage. My innate optimism gave me hope once more.

Snapping to attention, as I had done in Dęblin, I reported my name and the arrival of the Dęblin camp members. Without waiting for a reaction, I immediately said, “Herr Factory Security Chief, we have the word of honor of a German officer and a written document guaranteeing our protection. I hope the word of a colonel still holds and that nothing will happen to our children.” Thankfully, he did not interrupt me. Only after I finished speaking did he ask, “Where is this letter?” “Our transport leader, Rademacher, has it,” I answered immediately. Rademacher was summoned and handed the letter to Bartenschlager. He tore open the envelope, unfolded the letter, and read it—once, then again—shaking his head as we anxiously watched his face, awaiting his decision. I did not know what was written in the letter, so I was very surprised when Bartenschlager, after reading it, asked, “Wenkart, is your child among them? You can have yours, but the others must leave the camp.”

I protested, “Herr Factory Security Chief, this is not about my child. Either all or none! But if you do not return our children, then shoot me right now, because I was a fool to trust the word of a German officer.”

I saw that my words had an effect. Around me, unrest spread, and it seemed the guards were just waiting for an order to punish me for my audacity. Bartenschlager turned away without a word and ordered his deputy to take the men and then the women to their quarters. The children were to be housed separately outside the camp. No final decision was made about their fate.

Dejected, we followed our guards to our factory quarters. The only glimmer of hope in our bleak situation was that we knew our children were housed in a wooden hut outside the camp, surrounded by a garden. We could see them playing, and even exchange calls with them. One remark from Ruthi, who was not even six years old at the time, has stayed with me because it shows how serious-minded she was: “They wanted to give us tea, but we didn’t drink it—it could be poisoned!” What must have been going on in the minds of such children to have thoughts like that?

The effects of the separation varied. Some of the affected parents wailed loudly, while most were in a state of despair so deep it was almost manic. These desperate ones turned on me, spurred on by members of the first transport, who had also had their children taken away. Nothing was known about the fate of the sixteen children from that group, and they heaped unimaginable abuse on me. I endured it all without the slightest feeling of guilt.
Those were two bitter days for me. While I understood the parents’ anguish, their baseless accusations and foul insults wounded me deeply.

On the third day, as if by a miracle, the children were returned to us in the camp. The factory security chief’s wife was about to give birth, and the attending midwife had urged her to intervene so that the Jewish children would be returned to their parents.

I will never forget that day as long as I live. If my faith had ever wavered, this event made me believe again. Not only did the children’s return spark joy throughout the camp, but it also brought me personal relief.
Bartenschlager himself later told Jolles, the camp elder—loud enough for many to hear—“Only Wenkart’s intervention made me decide to let the children return to the camp.”

I do not believe that. More likely, his concern for his own newborn played a role. But I am certain that my actions helped ensure that the children were not taken away on the very day of our arrival, as had happened with the first transport.

The return of our children made us forget that on the very first day, we had been robbed of our belongings, subjected to body searches for hidden valuables, and even stripped of our outer garments. We were assigned to barracks wearing only skirts and trousers. Everything was filthy and poorly furnished. Only the infirmary, under the direction of a good doctor from Bialystok, was comparable to the conditions in Dęblin. The food was nowhere near as good—we received not even 700 calories a day. If not for bakers working in the city of Częstochowa who smuggled bread into the camp daily, many would have starved to death.

Two of my Viennese comrades, my deputy Großmann and the quartermaster Pollak, could not endure the hardships. They fell ill and died in Częstochowa. The camp inmates worked in the munitions factory, some at the machines, others transporting loads. In total, 2,000 people worked there, strictly guarded by the Ukrainian factory security force—Russian defectors who opposed Bolshevism and were not exactly friendly towards Jews. Their commander was Bartenschlager, assisted by Keutsch, who took pleasure in playing the tough man, strutting through the camp with a whip and lashing it down on a Jew’s back at the slightest provocation. Our children would hide without a trace the moment they heard that this sadist was coming.

In numerous conversations with long-time camp inmates, mostly from Kraków, our descriptions of the conditions in Dęblin were met with disbelief.
