Had they not seen with their own eyes our relatively healthy appearance, our clothing, and our luggage—which suggested a neutral foreign country rather than forced labor camps in the East—they would never have believed us.

It was heartbreaking to watch the work crews line up early in the morning, dressed in tattered clothes, their faces blackened with machine oil, emaciated by hunger, and then march into the factory halls, which were just as uninviting. We called these unfortunate souls the “Mongols.” The work hours were from 7 AM to noon and then from 1 PM to 6 PM—a total of ten hours a day, which was a long time given the inadequate nutrition. The only exception was Saturday, when work ended at noon, followed by cleaning duties in the camp and factory. The same happened on Sunday mornings. The only hours we had to ourselves were on Sunday afternoons. That was when conversations took place, bodies were given a more thorough cleaning, and—above all—plans were made.

What would happen when we were free? Each of us built castles in the air, imagined a wonderful life, and tried, through these illusions, to forget the cruel reality for a few hours. Precisely because we did not know if we would live to see the next day, precisely because everything was so hopeless, we planned—boldly and confidently—to keep from losing the last shred of our self-respect.

The exhausting work drained us so completely that after our meager evening meal, we would collapse onto our primitive bunks, staring blankly ahead. Few managed to sleep. The coming morning was too uncertain, the present too bleak, and the future too full of unanswered questions.

A recurring topic of conversation was the comparison between Częstochowa and Dęblin. The difference was stark! Both camps were not under the feared SS but rather under the Wehrmacht (Dęblin) and the factory security force (Częstochowa), which in turn answered to the civilian factory management.

Nevertheless, the difference was glaring. In my opinion, the blame lay with the factory security chief, Bartenschlager, as well as the Jewish camp elder, Jolles, who obediently carried out all orders. But I also held the factory director and his civilian foremen responsible. In Dęblin, many of the construction supervisors were always trying to improve and ease the suffering of the Jews. I remember names like Thiele, Gall, and Leistikov, as well as construction manager Figura and many department heads who treated us better than we ever experienced in Częstochowa.

Bartenschlager had introduced the kapo system, typical of concentration camps, through Jolles. These kapos were responsible for maintaining order in the camp. To their credit, I must say that I never heard any complaints about them during my time there.

I was assigned to the bathhouse, which also contained a large disinfection boiler. My task was to hang the dirty laundry of the factory workers in the boiling vat and, after disinfection, pass it on to the adjacent laundry facility. Once cleaned and repaired, the laundry was returned for exchange. This was only possible because the clothing of our transport had been confiscated and added to the depot. The bathhouse kapo had to enforce strict discipline, as there was constant conflict between the inmates from Częstochowa, whose clothing was already in tatters, and those from Dęblin, who had brought better garments. These disputes, as well as other minor issues, often escalated into loud arguments. Quelling these conflicts required great authority, which kapo Weiß possessed—even without being harsh.

Dipping and removing garments from the boiling disinfection solution, surrounded by the hot, suffocating steam, took a heavy toll on me. In the nearly six months I worked there, I lost almost twenty kilograms. That it wasn’t more, I owed to the selflessness of my wife.

She worked in the factory security kitchen as a potato peeler and cleaner. As a reward for her difficult work, she received a liter of stew containing meat, barley, potatoes, and a lot of bacon. She brought this as a supplement to the meager camp rations for my mother-in-law, Ruthi, and me. We always devoured the meal greedily—too late realizing that my wife was going without, claiming she had already eaten. Eventually, she grew so visibly thin that I protested. But she simply replied, “First, I must keep you alive. You must survive. A life without you is meaningless to me.” I was deeply moved by this declaration.

It was a terrible year, and then came January 15, 1945. We in the camp were more or less aware of the front lines, as a resistance group had formed under the leadership of our camp doctor, who had radio contact with the partisans.
It was said that the deportation of the camp into the interior of Germany was imminent, and indeed, the first transport was dispatched on January 15. It was sent to Buchenwald, with many casualties.

Soon after the first group of factory workers departed, we noticed unrest and uncertainty among our civilian supervisors. It was clear that they were thinking more about themselves than about work. Even the factory security force had lost much of its usual arrogance. We even noticed that Keutsch was no longer carrying his ever-present whip.

There was no longer any real work in the factory. Production had completely ceased, and the Mongols were put to work dismantling machines, while we—especially the resistance group—carefully observed the foremen’s mysterious activities. We rightly feared that the factory, with its abundant supply of explosives, would be blown up with us inside when the front approached.

In the camp office, transport lists were being prepared, and the deportation was being organized. Fortunately, since my wife worked for the factory security force, she and I were assigned to the last transport.
Great anxiety and fear gripped us when, on January 16, Ukrainian Waffen-SS troops arrived at the camp. We all feared the worst—liquidation. Everyone’s nerves were stretched to the breaking point.
The resistance group issued an order: if there was any sign of execution preparations, we were to fight back as hard as possible—there were weapons ready.

The emotions we felt for our wives and children can only be compared to those of a condemned man awaiting his execution. We looked up at the overcast sky, and it felt like our prayers were answered when, suddenly, an air raid alarm sounded and Russian planes appeared overhead. We were ordered into the barracks, while the Germans took cover in the bomb shelters.
The explosions of the falling bombs did not bother us inside the barracks, where a heated discussion began about what would happen next.

One decision was clear: we would delay every deportation as much as possible—to stall until the Russians arrived. And if danger arose, we would retreat into the barracks to avoid execution on the camp grounds.
It was an uncertain plan—but the next day, it would prove to be a brilliant one.

The bombardment lasted all day, and when the all-clear was given late in the evening, it was too late to organize the second transport. We received the food that had not been distributed at midday and were instructed to pack all our belongings and be ready for transport. I spent the seemingly endless night with my family—none of us could sleep, no matter how exhausted we were.

At last, dawn began to break. I left the barrack to visit the latrine and, at the same time, to see what was happening outside. My first glance was toward the bivouac of the Waffen-SS troops who had arrived the previous day. When I saw nothing, I naturally assumed they had positioned themselves somewhere, ready to receive us. I could not have known that what we had perceived as an aerial bombardment was already the thunder of artillery, signaling the proximity of the front lines.

The camp came to life. A fine drizzle fell. Keutsch, without his whip, went into all five barracks and said: “Everyone must form up by barrack for transport. Wagons are ready at the station. No more work will be done in Częstochowa.”
When asked about our destination, he said he believed it was Gleiwitz but was not sure.

The mere fact that he allowed the question and was even willing to answer it made us sense a change in the times. Who among us would have dared to ask Keutsch a question before? A whip blow would have been the inevitable response. Around nine in the morning, the first barrack was called up. Each name was read aloud, and the delayed responses of each man took up a lot of time. Just as they were finishing, and the second barrack began to be called, the inmates of the first barrack slowly shuffled back inside. The same thing happened when the third barrack was called. The other two barracks were never even reached. Despite Keutsch’s screaming and fits of rage, and the efforts of his obedient camp elder, the pattern kept repeating. As soon as one barrack was called outside and the names were read, everyone withdrew inside as soon as the next barrack was called. We seemed to obey, but Keutsch was driven almost mad.

The short winter day passed, and just as darkness fell, another air raid alarm sounded. The call of “Everyone into the barracks!” was music to our ears. I made plans with my family. If men and women were separated during transport, I felt obliged to arrange with a friendly couple from Prešov that Mrs. Wahrmann, a young and resolute woman, would take care of my family. I wanted to ease the burden on my wife, who had to care for my 75-year-old mother and six-year-old Ruthi. In return, I promised to look after Mr. Wahrmann, who was less agile.

Bombs fell near our factory complex, striking a nearby paper factory. We saw bright flames shoot up and heard explosions. In terror, we rushed outside, fearing the fire might spread to our wooden barracks. The factory itself, which was said to store an unknown quantity of explosives, also seemed at risk. We all remembered Keutsch’s words: the factory was rigged for destruction to prevent it from falling into Russian hands. That was supposed to make us more willing to march out. After the liberation, we learned that machine guns had been set up at the entrance gate, ready to mow us down at the slightest disturbance.

The Warta River flowed beside our barrack, and I had my family step into the water at the riverbank to escape the inferno. I myself went to the factory gate to see what was happening. It was night, the sky eerily red from the flames, and the constant crashing of explosions frayed our nerves. What a liberating feeling it was when we heard a loudspeaker announcement: “All Germans to the gate immediately!” We sensed that salvation from fear, terror, and mortal danger had finally arrived. Liberation seemed near.

Our hopes were strengthened when we saw our comrades wearing steel helmets and carrying machine pistols, led by the camp doctor holding a revolver. They drove the Ukrainian factory guards before them. A cry rang out from all lips: “We are free!” People embraced, tears streaming down their faces, without caring whether they knew each other or not. Comrades stormed the storage rooms and distributed bread and canned food to the starving prisoners.

On January 17, Red Army tank units entered the factory. We learned that the Germans had been surprised and attacked from the rear through a flanking maneuver, making it possible for a Jewish camp to be liberated without evacuation for the first time.
It is impossible to describe the elation that followed the liberation from barbaric oppression.

I hope my words have proven that every German, if they wished, could have acted humanely toward the persecuted and oppressed, even in the most precarious situations, without putting themselves in danger. Many may see my fate as an exception, but I believe it only further exposes the web of lies in which Nazi leaders ensnared the public. It was not some inevitable fate that led to this inferno, which defied all notions of culture and humanity. It was the willingness of so many to participate in the crimes, the sheer moral indifference, and the lack of will to act humanely that made it possible. The passivity of the indifferent, the collaboration of the willing, and the negligence of many—also from other nations—created the vacuum of humanity from which we have yet to fully recover.

No whitewashing, no denial, no so-called “coming to terms” can obscure this reality. Only a clear and unequivocal rejection of the perpetrators—whether those who carried out the crimes with their own hands, those who devised the ideological foundation from their desks, or those who issued the orders—can consign this shameful past to history. It will remain alive for as long as criminals from that time walk free and rise unpunished to high positions. Only the dying out of that cursed generation will truly close this chapter.

My examples should make it clear that there was no such thing as an unavoidable “necessity to follow orders” for leading Nazis. Whether an officer or a soldier in a camp, each had the ability to prevent atrocities in their own way. Those who shed blood did so willingly and with full knowledge of their actions. They knew that the mass murder of women, children, and the elderly—of innocent people—was wrong, that the persecution of Jews, Roma, and others declared enemies of the regime was a crime. Any remotely responsible German must have recognized the injustice of these acts, even if they were sanctioned by the authorities, and must have realized they bore responsibility.
Perhaps I am simplifying a highly complex matter, but I want to make one thing clear: these horrors were not an inescapable fate for those who became accomplices.

For those within my view, however, any claim to leniency has been forfeited—they should bear the full weight of the law. The claim that most of them “did not know what they were doing” is likely a postwar fabrication. The broad outlines of the truth were clear to nearly everyone. The Eichmann trial proved beyond doubt that the “Final Solution” was widely known, even before the Wannsee Conference, and was propagated in Nazi publications like Das Schwarze Korps.

At the very least, those who carried out the actions, eyewitnesses, and through them an even wider circle, knew about the mass murder of millions, the theft from countless victims, and the sheer lawlessness of these crimes.

I reject the idea of collective guilt, as I know from my own experience that many Germans did everything in their power to alleviate the suffering of the persecuted. But I cannot absolve many others of complicity. It is essential that all decent people clearly distance themselves from the perpetrators so that these crimes are never silently accepted—and never repeated.

We stayed in Częstochowa for a few more days, surviving on the camp’s supplies, which were now being distributed in a somewhat organized manner by a newly formed committee. The inactivity, the rumbling of the Stalin organs (rocket launchers), and the fear that we might fall back into the hands of the Germans drove me to leave the camp. My destination was Kraków, in the already liberated Polish hinterland. The odyssey of the eight-day, 128-kilometer march that I undertook with my wife is impossible to describe in its entirety, with all its hardships and dangers, so I will limit myself to the most striking episodes.

It must have been January 23rd when I set out, carrying three loaves of bread and two kilograms of soap from the factory warehouse. Due to the severe frost—about minus twenty degrees—my mother-in-law and child remained in the camp. In Kraków, I hoped to secure an apartment through the JUS (Jewish Support Office), from which I had previously received medical supplies for the Dęblin camp and which I still believed to exist. We lacked proper outer clothing. Instead of a coat, I wore a heavy scarf and a blanket; my wife wore a light coat with a camp blanket over it, made of a mixture of cotton and paper fibers, which provided little warmth. The strong wind whipped our faces, and our breath turned into tiny ice crystals the moment it left our mouths. Nevertheless, our spirits were not low. With every step, with every kilometer, we knew we were escaping the place of our captivity.

Since I did not speak Polish and speaking German was unwise, I memorized a few sentences in Russian to respond to the Russian patrols that appeared frequently. I always stated that I was a Jewish concentration camp prisoner to avoid trouble. When trucks carrying Russian soldiers stopped, I never dared to ask for a ride—partly because of my wife, who only spoke German, and partly because I sensed that my Jewish identity did not make me particularly welcome. Every time I used the Russian word for Jew, “Jiwree,” during an inspection, the initially friendly faces visibly darkened. This made me cautious, and I tried to avoid encounters altogether.

After about twenty kilometers of walking, we reached a small village in the evening. I went to the village elder, the “Wójt,” and asked for a place to stay. He sent us to a widow with a young daughter, who offered my wife and me a shared bed in the kitchen. The exceptionally warm welcome quickly made us forget the day’s hardships. We were immediately offered hot tea and given fragrant pork lard. After washing ourselves in warm water, we slept in a soft bed for the first time in nearly four years. We woke up rested the next morning, were given warm milk, and, in gratitude for their hospitality, gave them two bars of soap, which they lacked.

At our heartfelt farewell—the two women spoke German—they warned us about the AK (Armia Krajowa), the Polish national underground army, which killed Jews as bloodthirstily as the SS did. I later learned how justified this warning was. Many of my fellow prisoners from Dęblin fell victim to this murderous gang after liberation. To avoid them, we steered clear of industrial towns and instead followed the main road to Będzin. We had been told that the AK avoided highways because they feared Red Army patrols.

At nightfall, after a bitterly cold day with nothing but dry bread in our stomachs, we saw a lone farmhouse by the roadside. Despite a loudly barking giant dog, I bravely knocked on the door and asked the farmer for shelter for the night. After questioning us about where we came from and where we were going, he let us in and prepared a straw bed for us in the entrance hall. We also saw his wife, who, like him, was rather gruff and unfriendly. It was clear that we were not welcome, and only the grim cold kept them from turning us away. Exhausted, we sank into the straw and covered ourselves with our blankets. Soon, we fell into a restless sleep, from which I was awakened around midnight by strange noises.

When I opened my eyes, I was paralyzed with fear. The farmer entered the room with a long knife in his hand, followed by his wife carrying a bucket of boiling water. I gave my wife a rough nudge, convinced that they were about to kill us. I quickly wrapped my hand in the blanket, ready to defend ourselves, determined to sell our lives as dearly as possible. But my fighting resolve turned into a sigh of relief when the farmer explained that we could go back to sleep—he was on his way to the barn because a cow was giving birth. I followed him to the barn to see for myself but left soon after, not wanting to witness the birth.

In the morning, we were given milk and received two loaves of fresh bread and canned sausage from a traveling Red Army field bakery. After another exhausting march, we reached the town of Będzin, where we met other liberated Jews who welcomed us warmly. We were served a hearty stew with plenty of meat and noodles, tea, and had a long conversation about the past.

Our host’s family had spent three years hiding in an underground dugout in the forest. They had survived only with the help of Polish villagers who, in exchange for large sums of gold, provided them with food. Their life in a concealed forest trench was so full of danger that our time in the camp seemed almost bearable in comparison to their suffering. For days without sunlight, packed tightly in a tiny space filled with the stench of their own waste, without any real way to clean themselves—and this for years...

We stayed in Będzin for three days, as our hosts insisted we wait until the biting cold subsided. The four-person family lived in just one room and a small shop, simply furnished. Despite their own hardships, they took us in, fed us, and even gave us provisions for our journey—a true example of selflessness, especially considering that they had spent years isolated from the world, completely at the mercy of their greedy helpers.

Strengthened and rested, we set off again. The harsh frost had eased slightly, and the winter sun shone in a clear sky. That day, we covered more than thirty kilometers. The cold, the worry about our relatives still in Częstochowa, and the determination to escape the war zone drove us forward.

By evening, we reached a suburb of Kraków. At the entrance to the town, there was a Russian checkpoint. I was separated from my wife and brought before an officer, who interrogated me for two hours. They searched me and my belongings thoroughly and asked me the most absurd questions in German. At the end, the officer gave an order I did not understand, and I was placed in a small, windowless room where I had to spend the night alone.

In that prison, the most terrible thoughts ran through my mind—deportation to the Russian hinterland, even execution. I suspected a trap in the interrogation conducted in German, and only my often-emphasized social democratic background gave me hope. The highly intelligent Russian officer had asked me many questions about Marxism, but his inscrutable expression gave me no clue as to whether my knowledgeable answers had impressed him. My ignorance of military matters, such as the condition and morale of the German army, surely did not hurt me either.

The next morning, they brought me tea, bread, and jam, and soon after, I was led into a large common room—where my wife was waiting. We embraced, tears in our eyes, and left the command post with a Russian-issued travel permit. My wife had spent the night in the common room with about thirty people of both sexes. Unable to understand anyone due to the language barrier, she had spent the night in deep anxiety over my fate.

The fortunate outcome of this intermezzo quickened our steps, and by around one o’clock in the afternoon, we had completed the ten-kilometer journey into the city center. We stood in front of the JUS building. There were no signs outside indicating an office, which surprised me greatly, as I had imagined a large organization. After all, the Jewish Relief Agency was listed under the protection of the International Red Cross in Geneva on its letterhead and was responsible for distributing medication, Ovomaltine, Vigantol, powdered milk, and bandages to Jews in SS camps.

In the courtyard, I discovered a warehouse with a tiny office, apparently looted, as only empty crates and packaging remained. My optimism plummeted to zero, and I reproached myself severely for having subjected my wife and myself to such hardships in pursuit of a mere illusion. The disappointment was immense. Unable to make any further decisions and as it was already getting dark, I asked the janitor who had accompanied me through the warehouse for permission to spend the night in the office. He had referred me to the city’s social welfare office, where I intended to try my luck the next day.

He provided us with some hot tea, and we choked down liverwurst and dry bread as a meager supper in a rather dejected mood. In the dirty, unheated room, covered with coarse blankets and plenty of packing paper, we spent the night, dozing off occasionally, and were relieved when morning finally came.

The local relief committee did, in fact, assign us a well-furnished apartment that had been vacated by the Germans, already inhabited by fellow sufferers. We were given a room to ourselves, along with money, medication for my wife, and ample food, allowing us to spend the next few days without much worry. Unfortunately, my wife’s worsening illness made hospitalization necessary. We had to go to Katowice, as it was the only place where hospitals were still operational. Clinging to the footboard of an overcrowded tram, we made our way there. Everywhere I pleaded for help for my feverish wife, but I was turned away.

In utter despair, I approached a Catholic priest whom I recognized by his cassock and asked for advice. My account seemed to shake him, as he gave me tips that initially seemed implausible but later proved extremely useful. He advised me to go to the Russian guard in Bogucice, a suburb, and, accompanied by soldiers, demand my wife’s admission to a hospital run by nuns, as she would likely be turned away otherwise. For myself, he suggested that I claim to have a Christian parent and register at a Caritas camp, where I could wait out the war in relative safety in exchange for a little work.

I owe a great deal to this man, who seemed like a saving angel. Everything he suggested worked. A fearsome-looking Russian soldier with a machine gun slung over his shoulder accompanied me to the hospital, and the admitting doctor did not dare refuse us. After diagnosing my wife with a fever of 40.1°C, she was given a bath and taken to a hospital room. The scene on the staircase was heart-wrenching when my wife cried out in despair, “This is my end. I will not leave here alive!” My words of comfort were met with disbelief. I, too, had little hope, as the doctor told me that only good nutrition and air with less coal dust than that in Katowice could help her.

Relieved that my wife was now safely in a hospital bed, I went to Caritas, where I received clean shelter in an emergency barracks and sufficient food. My willingness to help soon caught the attention of the nuns in charge. They listened to my story and soon offered me a job as a kitchen helper for decent pay. I accepted immediately, as I wanted to work—not only to distract myself from my worries but also because my thoughts were constantly filled with fear for my wife, my motherin-law, and my child, who had remained in Częstochowa. The sparse information I received about them did little to ease my concern. Since I was stuck in Katowice, I asked a Polish acquaintance to inquire about their whereabouts. The news he brought was devastating.

A fire, apparently caused by overheating, had broken out in a barracks, and my mother-in-law had suffered a heart attack and was buried in the Częstochowa cemetery. Our orphaned daughter, Ruthi, had been taken to Łódź by a fellow inmate from Dęblin. Later, I managed to track down her address and brought her to me in Katowice.

My wife remained in the hospital for four weeks, but the time passed quickly, and I was able to speed up her recovery with vitamins. As a surprise, I had arranged for a private lodging with a Volksdeutscher, a favor from the kind-hearted nuns. It was just a small furnished room with a single window, but to us, it felt like a palace. It had a small iron stove that we fueled with coal from the mines, creating a cozy warmth that made us feel at home. Best of all, after many hardships, we were reunited with Ruthi.
The only cloud over our joy was my wife’s occasional severe asthma attacks, which we managed to alleviate with Asthmolsin injections. I soon learned how to administer them myself.

Around May 1, 1945, we read in the Polish newspapers that the Red Army was advancing and that Vienna was already free. Our longing to return home grew immense, especially after seeing an old Viennese film in a cinema a few days earlier. The sight of the Ringstraße, the Parliament, and City Hall moved us so deeply that tears streamed down our faces. We resolved to leave, despite our relatively comfortable life and the dangers we anticipated. It was no small undertaking, given the widespread insecurity.

One was at the mercy of marauding occupation soldiers, and even the newly formed Polish militia was no guarantee of safety. But our yearning for Vienna overcame all doubts. On the morning of May 1, we went to Katowice station to find a way to depart. The direct route via Oderberg-Prerau-Lundenburg was impossible, as German troops were still fighting in the Ostrava coal region, cutting off the route. Moreover, I wanted to avoid combat zones. Our only option was a long detour through free Slovakia via Krosno-Debrecen-Budapest-Bratislava.

We boarded a coal freight train despite the prohibition and traveled undisturbed until late evening. We spent the night in a small station’s waiting room and caught another freight train in the morning. During the journey, we witnessed a Hungarian soldier, a former German ally, being identified and summarily executed by Russian soldiers. They threw his body off the train without hesitation. We watched in horror, reflecting on how little a human life was worth in those times. Even he had a right to a trial.

Later, we were forced off the train ourselves but managed to board another soon after, arriving in Krosno on the Polish-Slovak border. There, a Jewish committee assigned us money and accommodation with a Jewish couple. However, my wife fell seriously ill again, suffering severe asthma attacks due to a bad cold. The doctor forbade further travel, and I often had to administer injections to relieve her breathing difficulties. It was a hard time, but with the help of kind people, we got through it.

Once she recovered, my wife insisted we continue our journey home as soon as possible. Luck was on our side. A Red Cross train carrying sick concentration camp survivors was about to depart for Debrecen. Without hesitation, I gathered our few belongings and jumped onto the already moving train at the last moment.

After a series of hardships, we finally crossed a pontoon bridge over the Danube and stepped onto Austrian soil. A Russian truck brought us to the Urania in Vienna’s first district. Our odyssey had ended. Despite the destruction in Vienna, our hearts were light.
A chapter of our lives, written in blood and tears, had finally closed.

blocks.caption: Emmanuel Stein, Maria Winter (later Stein) Horn ca 1900

blocks.text: By chance, I learned that Mr. Hermann Wenkart was being attacked by certain fellow camp inmates due to his role as a camp leader. However, I had the opportunity to closely observe Wenkart from August 1941 to January 15, 1944, and in the interest of truth, I must make the following sworn statement. I am prepared to testify to the accuracy of my statements before any forum at any time.

I was a construction clerk at the company Autheried, which in 1941 was carrying out work at the Deblin Air Base. In August 1941, during the construction of the barbed wire fence around the Jewish camp, I met Wenkart, who was working as a clerk. From the beginning, his way of acting stood out to me. His dedication to the Jews under his supervision, his care, and his camaraderie were so unparalleled that we, who were obligated to serve, were in awe of his courage and energy, especially given the immense danger to life that every Jew—especially those in leadership positions—faced under the General Government.

I personally witnessed Wenkart, at great personal risk, confront sadistic German officers, thereby preventing the mistreatment of Jews. Countless times, he rescued camp inmates from imprisonment, which would have put them in mortal danger.

During the deportation from Deblin on May 6, 1942, it was Wenkart who initiated a plan involving the site manager of the company, Engineer Kozak, to hide over 100 Jews, including three women, in a warehouse belonging to the company—thus saving them from the SS, who were deporting over 1,600 Jews (only those deemed able to work) to an unknown fate, possibly death.

In October 1941, the Luftwaffe construction management dissolved the Jewish camp, and Wenkart—who was highly respected by the entire company—was transferred, along with 102 Jews, into the employment of Autheried. Wenkart became a group leader and was responsible for payroll, work assignments, and the welfare of the Jewish workers.
Regarding his work with us until he took over as camp leader in July 1942, not only I but also the following Vienna-based individuals can testify:
Ing. Kozak, former construction manager (Vienna XVI, Friedmanngasse 1a) Maierhofer, worker (Vienna X, Troststraße 81) Hammermaier, worker (Vienna XIX, Heiligenstädter Straße 84/53/10)

Wenkart ensured that the Jewish workers under his supervision were treated humanely, which was unheard of in the entire Eastern region at the time. Not only that, but he also secured higher wages than prescribed by disguising them as piecework and overtime payments. Additionally, Wenkart successfully negotiated food subsidies for the Jewish workers from German and Polish authorities, which we had thought impossible.

His own work performance was not only exemplary and flawless in every way, but his dedication to his fellow sufferers was so extraordinary that we at the company often mentioned him as a shining example in the years that followed.

In October 1942, we were transferred to Slonim, but I remained in contact with the Deblin Jewish camp because, thanks to Wenkart’s initiative, I was able to help his Viennese comrades (Engel, Pollak, Grossmann, and Gärtler) by using my business trips to Vienna to deliver news and packages from Vienna to Deblin several times a year. During my frequent visits to the camp, I was repeatedly able to confirm that Wenkart risked his life time and again for the benefit of the Jews. I must express my astonishment that Wenkart is being attacked by anyone at all.

I am currently the elected chairman of the workers’ council at Autheried (which employs about 500 people) and was nominated by all three political parties. If irresponsible individuals, who may have themselves harmed the camp, are attacking a selfless person like Mr. Wenkart, I see it as my personal duty to take a stand against this.
I am prepared to testify to my statements in person and under oath at any time.
Julius Haczek
Vienna 20, Leystraße 23/16/4
