blocks.text: In recent years, numerous publications have been published that deal with the history of Jews in Deblin and, in detail, with the role of Herrmann Wenkart.
For additional context, I have taken the liberty of adding these to the book.
This text comes from the Journal of Holocaust Research, which is published in Haifa, Israel. It examines the events of the Deblin camp.
https://holocaustjournal.haifa.ac.il
Historical Background

In 1939, after the German occupation of Poland, the General Government was established under the leadership of Hans Frank. This territory was divided into four administrative districts, including Lublin. The Puławy region, where the Dęblin-Irena camp was located, had about 26,000 Jewish inhabitants before the war, spread across 14 towns.

Immediately after the occupation, the Jewish residents were forced into labor, often through brutal street raids in which people were captured and compelled to perform work that usually had no productive function but was primarily intended to humiliate them.

The Puławy district became a center of forced labor for its Jewish population. Following Hans Frank’s decree in October 1939, which mandated forced labor for all Jews aged 16 to 60, the Jewish Councils (Judenräte) took on the task of providing workers. They organized labor forces for various activities, including agricultural work and private businesses that profited from the exploitation of cheap Jewish labor.
Forced Labor and Extermination in the Puławy District

In 1939, after the German occupation of Poland, the so-called General Government was established under the leadership of Hans Frank. The territory was divided into four administrative districts: Warsaw, Radom, Kraków, and Lublin. The Lublin district was further subdivided into ten administrative units, including the Puławy district. Before the war, approximately 26,000 Jews lived in this district, spread across 14 towns and villages.

With the German occupation, Jewish residents were immediately subjected to forced labor, often through brutal and violent street arrests. The Jewish Councils (Judenräte) attempted to regulate these arbitrary arrests by negotiating with the German occupation authorities to organize the forced labor system. Initially, the tasks assigned were of little significance, such as street cleaning, digging, or carrying water. These tasks primarily served to exhaust the Jews both morally and physically.

With the decree of October 26, 1939, which mandated forced labor for all Jews between the ages of 16 and 60, the work became increasingly systematic and targeted. Jewish laborers were employed in agricultural enterprises, private businesses, and sometimes directly for the SS. Particularly in the years 1942–1943, the exploitation of Jewish labor became an important economic resource for the German occupation forces.
The Camp at Deblin-Irena Airfield

From 1942 to 1944, a forced labor camp was established at the Dęblin-Irena airfield, operating until the end of the German occupation in July 1944. This camp was one of the last to remain in operation until the end of the war, distinguishing it from other forced labor camps in the region, which were already dissolved in 1943. The conditions in the camp and the interactions between the Jewish workers and the German overseers are central themes of this chapter. Despite the harsh conditions, hundreds of Jews survived in this camp, in contrast to other camps in the region, from which only a few survived.

The camp at the airfield was unique because it was controlled by the German Air Force (Luftwaffe), and the Jewish workers were primarily responsible for the construction and maintenance of airfields. This work was considered crucial for the war effort, which may have been a factor in the survival of many Jewish laborers.
This chapter examines the conditions of forced labor and the impact of relationships between Jews and their oppressors on survival during this difficult time.
Chapter 3: The Camp Administration in Deblin-Irena

The forced labor camp at the Dęblin-Irena airfield was characterized by its internal organization and leadership structures, which were largely managed by Jewish prisoners themselves. A particularly important figure was Hermann Wenkart, who served as the Lagerältester (camp elder). Wenkart, an Austrian of Jewish origin, was deported to Opole in 1941 and later played a central role in the administration of the Dęblin-Irena camp.

Wenkart was a former lawyer and had a good relationship with certain German officers, including one whose life he had saved during World War I. Thanks to these connections, he was promoted to leadership positions, allowing him to oversee the distribution of food and medicine and arrange for wages to be paid to workers. These responsibilities enabled him to maintain some level of control over the lives of the prisoners.
Internal Administration of the Camp

In addition to Wenkart, other Jewish inmates also played key roles in the camp’s administration. There was a Jewish police force, primarily composed of young men, responsible for maintaining order within the camp. Many of these men were regarded as ruthless and brutal in their treatment of fellow prisoners. Some reports emphasize that these Jewish policemen worked closely with Wenkart and used their influence to gain privileges.

Wenkart’s wife was also involved in the camp’s administration and managed the camp kitchen. This role provided certain advantages for her and her family, as kitchen work was considered less physically demanding.
Criticism of Wenkart

Although Wenkart helped many survive in the camp, his role was controversial. Some inmates harshly criticized him, accusing him of collaborating with the Germans and making decisions that endangered the lives of fellow prisoners. Particularly in relation to the deportations, he was accused of not doing everything in his power to save more people.

A key accusation was that on October 15, 1942, during the liquidation of the Dęblin-Irena ghetto, Wenkart allegedly prevented some Jews from entering the camp, which ultimately led to their deaths. Such incidents created tensions between Wenkart and other Jewish inmates.

Despite this criticism, Wenkart remained an influential figure in the camp, closely cooperating with the German leadership. However, it is emphasized that he often faced moral dilemmas, having to make decisions that affected either a smaller or a larger group of people.

The living conditions in the Dęblin-Irena forced labor camp were extremely harsh, as in most Nazi labor camps. The camp was surrounded by barbed wire and divided into five zones, each with different functions: there were living quarters for men and women, a section for children, as well as functional buildings such as warehouses and a bathhouse. Hygiene conditions in the camp were extremely poor, and diseases such as typhus and dysentery were widespread.
Work in the Camp

The prisoners were forced to work under extremely difficult conditions. The workday began early in the morning and typically lasted 12 hours. The Jewish inmates were primarily tasked with the construction and maintenance of the nearby airfield. Their duties included building runways and other construction work deemed essential for the war effort by the German Air Force (Luftwaffe).
Nutrition and Living Conditions

Food in the camp was inadequate. The prisoners received barely enough to sustain themselves through the grueling physical labor. While food rations were occasionally distributed, they were often insufficient. Due to the lack of nutrition and the harsh working conditions, the health of the inmates deteriorated rapidly. Many suffered from malnutrition and physical weakness, which further affected their ability to work.

Despite the poor living conditions, the inmates tried to maintain a sense of normalcy. There were educational and religious activities in the camp, and prisoners supported one another as best they could. These social and cultural activities helped them sustain their will to survive, even as they constantly faced the threat of deportation and death.
Chapter 5: Survival Strategies in the Deblin-Irena Camp

In the Dęblin-Irena forced labor camp, Jewish prisoners developed a variety of strategies to survive under extremely harsh conditions. Despite the daily threats of violence, malnutrition, and grueling labor, many inmates found ways to maintain their physical and mental well-being.
Physical Survival Strategies

particularly with the camp leader, Hermann Wenkart. Wenkart played an ambivalent role in the camp: while some prisoners criticized him for collaborating too closely with the Germans, others acknowledged that his connections with German officers helped secure better living conditions for the inmates. Thanks to these relationships, prisoners in Dęblin-Irena received relatively better food and clothing compared to other camps. Additionally, Wenkart attempted to procure coal for heating through bribery, helping protect prisoners from the harsh winter conditions.

Furthermore, prisoners relied on mutual support and solidarity to survive physically. Work was often distributed among them, with stronger individuals assisting the weaker ones. This collaboration helped many endure the daily hardships.
Psychological Survival Strategies

In addition to physical strategies, inmates also developed psychological methods to cope with the constant fear and uncertainty. Religious and cultural activities played a significant role in camp life. Despite the dire circumstances, prisoners continued to hold religious services and celebrate Jewish holidays. These activities provided a sense of normalcy and unity.

Educational activities were also organized to provide intellectual stimulation. This not only strengthened the prisoners’ will to survive but also offered a means of escaping the daily brutality by focusing on learning.
Escape Attempts

Some prisoners attempted to escape the realities of the camp by fleeing. However, escape attempts were extremely dangerous, and many who tried were captured and executed. Nevertheless, some inmates took the risk, especially in the final years of the war when German control weakened and the front lines drew closer.
Conclusion

The survival strategies in the Dęblin-Irena camp were diverse and reflected the resilience and adaptability of the Jewish prisoners. Despite the constant threats of violence and death, many found ways to endure both physically and mentally. The role of Hermann Wenkart and other camp officials remained controversial, but their ability to negotiate better living conditions played a crucial role in the survival of many inmates in the camp.
Chapter 6: Cooperation and Conflicts in the Deblin-Irena Camp

The relationship between Jewish prisoners and their German overseers in the Dęblin-Irena camp was marked by constant tension but also by instances of cooperation. Many inmates had to interact with their German overseers to improve their chances of survival, which often led to moral dilemmas.
Collaboration and Moral Dilemmas

Hermann Wenkart, the camp elder, played a particularly controversial role. He closely cooperated with German officers and attempted to improve camp conditions through negotiations and bribery. This allowed him to secure better living conditions for some prisoners, though often at the expense of others. While some inmates saw his actions as necessary for survival, others accused him of supporting the Germans and betraying fellow Jewish prisoners.

Jewish inmates involved in administrative roles within the camp, such as the Jewish police, were also heavily criticized. Many prisoners viewed them as particularly brutal and ruthless. Some of these policemen abused their power to gain personal advantages, further deepening the divisions within the camp.
Social Tensions Among Prisoners

In addition to conflicts with the overseers, there were also significant tensions among the Jewish inmates themselves. The camp housed different groups, including local Jews from the region and Jewish refugees from Slovakia. These groups often found themselves in competition, particularly over scarce resources like food and clothing.

The Slovakian Jews formed a particularly close-knit community and showed strong solidarity with one another. They also received aid from outside the camp in the form of relief packages sent by Jewish organizations in Slovakia. However, this support intensified tensions with Polish Jews in the camp, who had no access to similar aid and often saw the Slovakian group as privileged.

Life in the Dęblin-Irena camp was shaped by a complex web of cooperation and conflict. While some prisoners sought to improve their survival chances through collaboration with the Germans, this often deepened tensions with other inmates, who were locked in a constant struggle for limited resources. Hermann Wenkart’s role remained particularly controversial, as not all saw his efforts to ensure survival in the camp as morally justified.
Chapter 7: The Dissolution of the Deblin-Irena Camp

The Dęblin-Irena camp was dissolved in 1944 as the German army was forced to retreat due to the advancing Soviet forces. The dissolution occurred in several phases, beginning in May 1944, when the first groups of prisoners were deported to other camps.
Escape Attempts and Resistance

During the camp’s dissolution, there were several escape attempts. Some prisoners tried to flee into the nearby forests, hoping to join partisan groups or simply to survive. However, many of these escape attempts ended in tragedy, as most fugitives were either captured by German guards or killed by local militias. Nevertheless, a small number of prisoners managed to escape successfully and went into hiding.
The Final Evacuation

In July 1944, as the front line drew closer, the camp was completely evacuated. The remaining prisoners were loaded onto trains and deported to other concentration camps, including Częstochowa and Auschwitz. These deportations marked the end of the Dęblin-Irena camp. Many of the prisoners who were sent to these camps did not survive the following months, as they were either murdered in the gas chambers or perished under the catastrophic conditions of the war’s final phase.

The dissolution of the Dęblin-Irena camp was part of the broader retreat of the German army from occupied territories in Eastern Europe. Despite some prisoners’ efforts to escape or resist deportation, the fate of most inmates ended in tragedy. Only a small group survived the final phase of the war—either by fleeing or by enduring the harsh conditions of the concentration camps.
Chapter 8: Post-War Period and Memories

After the end of World War II, the survivors of the Dęblin-Irena camp began to process their experiences and share them with the world. This chapter focuses on the memories of the survivors and their efforts to preserve the history of the camp and the forced labor at the Dęblin airfield.
Returning Home

Many survivors returned to their hometowns after the war, only to find that their world had been completely transformed. The Jewish population in Poland had been almost entirely wiped out by the Holocaust, and those who returned often found no surviving relatives. Their homes had been destroyed or taken over by others, leaving them with the difficult task of rebuilding their lives.
The Role of Memories

The memories of the survivors played a crucial role in coping with their traumatic experiences. Many survivors published memoirs detailing their time in the camp and their forced labor experiences. These accounts remain invaluable in documenting the scale of the atrocities committed against Jews in Poland.
Commemorations and Memorials

Over the years, several commemorative events have been held to honor the victims of the Dęblin-Irena camp. Memorials have been erected at various locations, including former forced labor camp sites. These memorials serve not only as places of remembrance but also as a warning to future generations to never forget the horrors of the Holocaust.

This final chapter highlights the importance of remembrance after the Holocaust and the survivors’ efforts to pass on their experiences. These memories are an essential part of Holocaust history and help ensure that the victims are never forgotten.
Chapter 9: Conclusions and Outlook
This chapter summarizes the key insights from the history of the Dęblin-Irena forced labor camp and provides an outlook on its historical significance within the context of the Holocaust.
The Significance of the Dęblin-Irena Camp

The Dęblin-Irena forced labor camp was one of many camps established during World War II to exploit the Jewish population in Poland. Its particular significance lies in the fact that it remained operational until shortly before the end of the German occupation, playing a central role in the German labor program—especially in supporting the war effort through the construction and maintenance of the Dęblin airfield.
Survival Through Forced Labor

Many Jewish prisoners in the Dęblin-Irena camp survived the Holocaust due to the strategic importance of the airfield. Forced labor in the aviation industry was considered vital by the German authorities, which extended the camp’s operation and, consequently, the survival of its inmates. This explains why some prisoners had a better chance of survival compared to those in other camps.
Collaboration and Resistance

The ambiguous role of individuals like Hermann Wenkart, the camp elder, remains a controversial topic. On one hand, his negotiations with German officers helped secure the survival of many prisoners; on the other hand, these collaborations were often morally questioned. The experiences in the Dęblin-Irena camp highlight the difficult choices Jewish inmates had to make in order to survive.
Outlook

The history of the Dęblin-Irena camp serves as a powerful example of the brutal exploitation and resistance of the Jewish population during the Holocaust. It underscores the necessity of preserving and sharing such stories to keep the memory of the victims alive and to ensure that such atrocities never happen again.

With these conclusions, the study of events at the Dęblin-Irena camp comes to an end, but the lessons learned from it remain relevant for understanding humanity, morality, and survival in extreme conditions.
Bombing of the Deblin Airfield on September 3, 1939

Before World War II, thousands of Jews lived in the towns of Dęblin and Irena [pl][a] in central Poland; Irena had been home to the Polish Air Force Academy since 1927. In September 1939, the town was captured during the German invasion of Poland, and the persecution of Jews began with forced recruitment for forced labor and the establishment of a Judenrat (“Jewish Council”).

In November 1940, a ghetto was established in Irena, initially consisting of six streets. It was an open ghetto (Jews were not allowed to leave without permission, but non-Jews were permitted to enter). Many ghetto residents worked on labor projects for the Dęblin Fortress (a German Army base), the railway, and the Luftwaffe.

From May 1941, Jews from the ghettos of Opole and Warsaw were sent to labor camps around Dęblin. Conditions in the ghetto worsened at the end of 1941 due to increasing German restrictions and epidemics of typhus and dysentery.

The first deportation took place on May 6, 1942, sending about 2,500 Jews to the Sobibór extermination camp. A week later, two thousand Jews from Slovakia and hundreds more from nearby liquidated ghettos arrived.[b] In October of the same year, the Irena Ghetto was liquidated; about 2,500 Jews were deported to the Treblinka extermination camp, while about 1,400 Jews were kept in forced labor camps in the town.

Unusually, the labor camp operated by the Luftwaffe—holding around a thousand Jews at its peak—remained in existence until July 22, 1944, less than a week before the Red Army took over the region. As one of the last Jewish labor camps in the Lublin district, it allowed hundreds of Jews to survive the Holocaust.
Some survivors who returned to their homes were met with hostility, and several were murdered; all had left the region by 1947.
Background
Jews in Dęblin welcomed Polish Marshal Józef Piłsudski after the capture of Dęblin during the Polish-Soviet War of 1920.

Dęblin and Irena [pl][a] (Yiddish: מאדזשיץ, Modzhitz) are located 70 kilometers northwest of Lublin, Poland, at the confluence of the Vistula and Wieprz rivers and at an important junction of the Lublin– Warsaw railway line.

Local Jews supported the January Uprising of 1863. By the late 19th century, the area had become a center of Hasidism; most Jews followed the Modzitz dynasty, named after the town of Irena. There were also Gur Hasidim. The Modzitz Rebbe, Yisrael Taub, settled in the town in 1889.

Other Jews were Bundists or Zionists. Many earned their livelihoods as peddlers, shopkeepers, or craftsmen (especially in leather and metalworking) and later contributed to the town’s development as a summer resort.
In 1927, the civilian population of Dęblin and Irena was 4,860, including 3,060 Jews. From 1927, Dęblin was home to the Polish Air Force Academy, whose airfield was one of the largest in Poland.
German Invasion

During the German invasion of Poland, which marked the beginning of World War II in Europe, the Luftwaffe bombed Dęblin between September 2 and 7, 1939, targeting the military airfield, Polish Army ammunition depots, and a nearby bridge over the Vistula River. Most Polish soldiers left the city on September 7; on September 11, the remaining Polish forces blew up the ammunition storage facilities and withdrew. The German army arrived on either September 12 or 20. Many residents, both Jews and other locals, had fled the air raids. In the following weeks, most returned under German pressure. The Jews found that their homes had been looted. They were forced into labor groups to clear bomb damage and repair some buildings. Additionally, the Jewish community was fined 20,000 złoty.

During the German occupation, Dęblin and Irena became part of the Lublin District in the General Government. By the end of 1939, Jews were required to wear armbands with the Star of David, and some were forcibly sent to labor camps in Janiszów, Bełżec, and Pawłowice.

Later that year, the German occupiers forced the Jews to establish a Judenrat (Jewish Council) to mediate between the Jewish community and German demands. The Judenrat in Dęblin was formed later than in other regions and had its office on Bankowa Street. Its first members tried to mitigate the impact of German demands and warn residents about searches. The first chairman, Leizer Teichman, was dismissed in early 1941; reports suggest that he and his secretary, along with their families, were deported to Wąwolnica after attempting to bribe police officers. The Judenrat was temporarily led by Kalman Fris, who resigned after a few months. In August, he was replaced by Vevel Shulman, and in September 1941, by a man named Drayfish from Konin.
The Ghetto

In November 1940 or early 1941, a Nazi ghetto was established in Irena on the orders of Kurt Lenk, the region’s new land commissioner. Some Jews were forced to leave their homes and move into the ghetto. The likely motive was to keep Jews away from two nearby military facilities: a Luftwaffe airfield and a German army base filled with troops preparing for the 1941 invasion of the Soviet Union. The official decree for the ghetto’s establishment was issued only in 1942.

Until 1942, Irena was an open ghetto (there was no physical fence, but leaving without permission was punishable by death), consisting of six streets. Its boundaries included Okólna Street, the Irenka River, Bankowa Street, and Staromiejska Street. In September 1942, Staromiejska Street was removed from the ghetto, and its residents were relocated to Bankowa Street. Initially, non-Jews were allowed to enter the ghetto and even live there, allowing many Jews to survive by bartering goods for food. A secular Polish-language school, led by Aida Milgroijm-Citronbojm, educated 70 to 100 students.

The Jewish ghetto police maintained order inside the ghetto, while outside it was guarded by a special service unit made up of Volksdeutsche (ethnic Germans) and Ukrainian auxiliary police. This unit was notorious for beating, humiliating, and killing ghetto residents.

By late 1941, non-Jews were prohibited from entering the ghetto, leading to an increase in waste and epidemics of typhus and dysentery. The sick were treated in a 30-bed hospital run by Isaar Kawa, a doctor from Konin. With the help of non-Jewish doctors, medicine was smuggled in from Warsaw. At the same time, the Germans tightened restrictions: the use of ovens was banned, winter clothing was confiscated, and fuel imports were prohibited.

More Jews began leaving the ghetto in search of essentials, leading to the execution of twenty young women who were caught outside. Due to relatively better conditions in the Lublin District compared to Warsaw, some Jews fled to towns like Irena, but twenty unregistered Jewish refugees were executed.
Forced Labor

Until the end of 1942, Jews received wages for forced labor, except for those recruited by the city administration for tasks such as street cleaning and snow removal. Some Jews worked for German companies like Schwartz and Hochtief, which were contracted by the Wehrmacht for construction work at military bases in the city.

The Eastern Railway (Ostbahn) hired Schultz to upgrade the Lublin– Warsaw rail line, a project that employed 300 Jews starting in June 1941. Working conditions on this site were extremely harsh, with Jews forced to work twelve-hour days.

Most Jews, however, worked for the German army or the Luftwaffe; women and children grew food for the military. At the Dęblin Fortress, which had been taken over by the German army and where about 200 Jews from the ghetto worked, Stalag 307 was established on July 10, 1941—a prisoner-of-war camp for Soviet soldiers. Approximately 80,000 Soviet prisoners were held there, all of whom died within months due to starvation and disease. The Jews working there witnessed that the prisoners endured even worse conditions than they did.

The Jews from Dęblin and Irena sought the best labor assignments; 200 of the Slovak deportees eventually worked for the city administration. Another 200 Slovaks later worked for Schultz after the company expanded its operations. Survivors reported that the German soldiers overseeing the forced laborers treated them relatively well, while some Polish supervisors beat the Jewish workers. The Ukrainian guards at the railway construction site were particularly brutal, frequently threatening workers with death.

In May 1941, in preparation for the invasion of the Soviet Union, the Luftwaffe contracted the Autheried company to expand the airfield. They recruited 200 Jewish workers from Opole, many of whom were Austrian Jews deported from Vienna in February 1941 who had sought to escape the harsh conditions in Opole. These Jews, along with 400 others recruited in Irena and at the Lipowa 7 camp in Lublin, as well as 500 Polish workers, worked twelve-hour shifts leveling runways, building roads, walls, air-raid shelters, and earth fortifications. They also constructed a barbed-wire-enclosed area near the runway for craftsmen.

After the project was completed in October, the workers were reassigned to various construction sites in the city, possibly related to a typhus epidemic. Some of the Jews from Vienna remained at the airfield because they had obtained work permits to help transport gravel and build fuel tanks. These workers formed the core of the later labor camp.

After the first deportation from the Irena ghetto on May 6, 1942, additional Jews sought work permits—often through bribery—to avoid deportation. By October 1942, the camp had 543 registered residents.

At that time, there were five forced labor camps around Dęblin: the Luftwaffe camp, the fortress, the railway station (with 300 Jews), the Lipowa Street camp (with 200 Jews and ethnic Poles), and another near the city limits with 300 Jews.
Deportation, Murder, and Liquidation
First Deportation

The first deportation took place on May 6, 1942, to make room for Slovak deportees who arrived a week later. A unit consisting of German and Ukrainian police, along with some German SS and Ukrainian SS auxiliary troops, ordered the Jews to assemble at the main square at 9:00 AM. According to Yad Vashem, the Blue Police (Polish police under German control) also participated in the action.

Probably because the Luftwaffe was trying to recruit more forced laborers, the Jews were forced to wait for five hours. At 2:00 PM, 1,000 Jews who already had work permits were separated, and local employers began selecting an additional 200–500 Jews. Around 40 or 50 people were killed while trying to join the labor group. Around 2,300 to 2,500 Jews from Dęblin—mainly elderly people and families with young children—were marched approximately 2.5 kilometers to the Dęblin train station at 6:00 PM and deported to the Sobibór extermination camp. The victims were beaten, and those who could not keep up the pace were shot. Only late at night were the Jews who had been assigned to labor allowed to return home. Every family was affected. After the deportation, the bodies of the dead were collected in the synagogue and transported out of the ghetto in carts.

Over the next two days, more than 4,000 Jews from the northern Puławy district were deported—200 from Bobrowniki (May 6), 1,800 from Ryki (May 7), 125 from Stężyca (May 7), 1,500 from Baranów (May 8), and 500 from Łysobyki (May 8)—and sent to Sobibór. This was part of a nationwide extermination operation in the Puławy district, the first in a series of systematic deportations in the Lublin district as part of “Operation Reinhard.” The district administrator, Alfred Brandt, ordered the operation shortly after Sobibór became operational; he was present during the Dęblin deportation.
Second Deportation

The next deportation, coinciding with the liquidation of the last two remaining ghettos in Puławy (Opole and Końskowola), took place on October 15, 1942. The day before, all laborers were ordered to remain at their workplaces. Under the command of SS-Obersturmführer Grossman, the remaining Jews were rounded up by gendarmes, Luftwaffe soldiers, and auxiliary police. Many Jews attempted to escape the roundup and reach the Luftwaffe labor camp, which at that time housed 543 registered residents. Some were turned away by the camp’s Jewish police. Israeli historian Talia Farkash estimates that around 500 Jews, including 60–90 young children, managed to enter the camp, mostly through bribery. Historian David Silberklang estimates that about 1,000 Jews entered the camp, including 280 women and 100 children.

Many of the Slovak Jews, unaware of what awaited them, remained in the ghetto while gathering their belongings. Between 215 and 500 Jews were shot by the Ukrainians and Germans while their homes were being cleared. Between 2,000 and 2,500 people—mostly Slovaks—were deported to the Treblinka extermination camp. Members of the Judenrat, the Jewish police, and their families—totaling around 100 people—were kept behind to clean up the ghetto. Possibly another 100 Jews remained hidden in the ghetto without permission. On October 28, the remaining Jews were sent to the Schultz forced labor camp, and the ghetto was officially liquidated. Either on that day or in the following weeks, 1,000 people from the labor camps were removed, including 200 workers from the Dęblin fortress and 200 to 500 Luftwaffe workers. They were deported to Końskowola or Treblinka.

After this deportation, Farkash estimates that about 1,400 Jews remained in the labor camps around Irena. Estimates suggest that 1,000 to 2,000 were in the Luftwaffe camp, another 300 in a camp near the train station, and 120 in another camp near the railway loading dock. In May or July 1943, most of the remaining prisoners in the railway camps were deported to the Poniatowa concentration camp; the rest were deported by the end of 1943. Hundreds of Jews were still living in the remaining labor camps in the Puławy district, but they were murdered during “Operation Harvest Festival” (November 2–3, 1943). This operation also killed the prisoners of Poniatowa but did not affect the Jews in the Luftwaffe camp in Irena, who were unaware of it. After “Operation Harvest Festival,” ten labor camps remained in the Lublin district, with more than 10,000 Jews still alive.
Luftwaffe Camp
Camp Leader

The senior prisoner (Lagerältester) at the Luftwaffe camp was Hermann Wenkert, a Jewish lawyer from Vienna who was deported to Opole in 1941 and later arrived in Irena with a group of volunteers. According to Wenkert’s account, soon after his arrival, he encountered Eduard Bromofsky, an Austrian Luftwaffe officer with whom he had served during World War I. Bromofsky convinced the camp commander to promote Wenkert. Even after Bromofsky was transferred, Wenkert retained his position due to his good relations with the German authorities.

Wenkert staffed the camp administration with other Viennese Jews, secured extra rations for himself and his family, and arranged for his family to be transferred from Opole to Irena. Due to his close ties with the German authorities and his use of his position for personal gain, some survivors viewed him as a collaborator. Whenever possible, Wenkert negotiated with the authorities and paid bribes to avoid punishments. In cases unknown to the Germans, he consulted respected prisoners. However, when he believed he had no other choice and the survival of the camp was at stake, Wenkert handed over Jews accused of offenses to the Germans, knowing that this meant their deaths. German-speaking Jews had a more favorable opinion of him than local Yiddish-speaking Jews. According to historian David Silberklang, both perspectives on Wenkert might be true: he sought personal advantages but also worked to keep other Jewish prisoners alive.
Conditions

Compared to other camps, conditions in the Luftwaffe camp were relatively good. Prisoners received food rations of about 1,700 calories per day (enough for survival), had access to weekly baths and medical care, and were allowed to practice their religion. The presence of 100 young children—justified by the claim that they increased their parents’ productivity—was particularly unusual and was not seen in any other camp in the Lublin district after Aktion Erntefest (Operation Harvest Festival). Aida Milgroijm-Citronbojm continued running the Polish-language school she had established in the ghetto within the camp. The SS, unaware of the children’s presence, conducted regular unannounced inspections, during which the children had to be hidden. According to historian Talia Farkash, Wenkert’s good relationship with the camp administration partly contributed to the relatively favorable conditions.

Wenkert managed to exempt religious Jews from working on the Sabbath and allowed the establishment of a Chevra Kadisha (burial society) to ensure that the dead were buried according to Jewish law. In 1943, about 80 Jews, including Wenkert, received kosher food for Passover.

The camp had three German commanders, all with the rank of Feldwebel (sergeant): Kattengel (until March 1943), Dusy, and Rademacher (during the last two months). While Kattengel was viewed with suspicion by prisoners because he patrolled with a dog and a whip, Dusy and Rademacher were described positively by survivors. Dusy even arranged for a woman to receive treatment at a German hospital after she was severely injured. Wiszniewski, a local ethnic German (Volksdeutscher), was the lead foreman overseeing Jewish workers and was not known for mistreating them. Autheried engineer Kozak provided food to the Jews he worked with.

The Slovak Jews in the camp were the last large surviving group of the nearly 40,000 Slovak Jews deported to the Lublin district in 1942. Jews remaining in Prešov hired couriers (non-Jews from the Polish-Slovak border region) to regularly bring letters and valuables into the camp. A committee was formed within the camp to distribute these goods among the Slovak Jews. However, cases of theft by Polish Jews led to tensions between the two groups.

Despite the relatively good conditions, some Jews attempted to escape, fearing the camp would be liquidated. The Luftwaffe camp administration imposed collective punishments to deter escapes. Both theft and possession of foreign currency were punishable by hanging. Punishments could be excessive and arbitrary—nine Jews were executed by a firing squad for accidentally starting a fire—but overall, conditions were less brutal than in other Nazi camps.
Liquidation

The camp was dissolved on July 22, 1944, the same day the Red Army reached Lublin. At the time of the deportation, about 800 to 900 Jews remained in the camp, including 400 to 600 from Dęblin or Irena, 40 from Vienna, and 70 to 80 from Slovakia. Two or three transports left Irena on July 17 and 22 (Silberklang mentions a third on July 20).

Since no one volunteered, the first transport consisted of about 200 people who had few connections in the camp, including single adults and fifteen children between the ages of three and six. On the orders of the camp’s security officer, Georg Bartenschlager, the fifteen children were shot upon arrival in Częstochowa. The remaining prisoners, including Wenkert and 33 young children, left with the second transport alongside the German camp commander, Rademacher, who carried a letter of protection from the airfield commander.
Escape and Deportation

Around fifty Jews managed to escape during the camp’s evacuation, but most of them were killed. The second transport arrived in Częstochowa on July 25; the young children were separated for two and a half days but were not killed. The reason for this remains unclear, though it was likely due to negotiations with Bartenschlager.

In Częstochowa, the Jews worked in the four Hugo Schneider AG (HASAG) factories. Some were deported to Buchenwald and Bergen-Belsen in January 1945, while others were liberated by the Red Army. About half of them survived.
A Unique Case in Nazi-Occupied Poland

According to historian Talia Farkash, the Luftwaffe camp at the Irena airfield was a “singular case” in the Lublin district and possibly in all of Nazi-occupied Poland. One unusual aspect of the camp was that it was operated by the Luftwaffe from beginning to end, rather than the SS, despite the SS attempting to expand its control in 1943 and 1944. According to David Silberklang, the fact that the Wehrmacht, rather than the SS, ran the camp played a crucial role in its continued existence. However, other Luftwaffe-run camps, such as three in the Zamość district, were liquidated before the end of 1943.

Another reason for the camp’s survival was the importance of Jewish labor for the German war effort in Dęblin, a point that Wenkert repeatedly emphasized in his negotiations with the Germans. Overall, luck also played a significant role, along with the combination of Jewish leadership, relatively lenient German overseers, and the evacuation of the camp to Częstochowa instead of Auschwitz-Birkenau.
While 99% of the Jews from the Lublin district were murdered in the Holocaust, hundreds from Dęblin and Irena survived the war.
Post-War Period
Dęblin was liberated by the 1st Polish Army on July 27, 1944. In 1945, Alfred Brandt was convicted of his crimes by a Soviet military tribunal near Słupsk and executed by firing squad.

Some Jewish survivors who attempted to return home in January 1945 were warned by the local chief of the Milicja Obywatelska (People’s Militia) that settling in the town was illegal. In March 1945, the surviving Luksemburg sisters—Łaja, Gitla, and Fryda—were murdered with the involvement of local policemen. Other Jews received threatening letters.

Following the Kielce Pogrom in 1946, a memorial gathering was held in Dęblin to honor the victims, but local residents disrupted the event with antisemitic slogans. By 1947, the last remaining Jew had left the town, and as of the 21st century, no Jewish community remains there. In 2011, the former synagogue in Dęblin was in a state of disrepair and was under discussion for demolition. In 2015, a memorial plaque was unveiled in Dęblin to commemorate the Irena Ghetto.
Ignaz Bubis, a former prisoner of the ghetto, served as chairman of the Central Council of Jews in Germany from 1992 to 1999.
Sam Harris lost his childhood due to Holocaust. He ensures that a new generation of young people can thrive.

Sam Harris is passionate about this cause. Despite being 88 years old and using a walker, he will personally greet you at the somber, industrial entrance of the museum he helped create—an institution that ensures the horrific yet essential history he lived through will continue to be told, even long after he is gone, and his voice, one of the last direct eyewitness accounts, has fallen silent.
But this was not always the case.

For many years, Harris—a former insurance professional and a member of the Northbrook, Illinois Rotary Club—refused to speak about how the Nazis had taken him away as a little boy. He did not want to talk about the unbearable hunger, the fear, the machine guns, his murdered parents, the cattle cars, or the concentration camps. To him, it was an old story, a distant past. Why bring it up? He was an American now. First, an American boy growing up in Northbrook, a comfortable suburb north of Chicago. Then, an American man who could choose what he wanted to talk about—or what he preferred to keep to himself.

His silence lasted for many years. “I knew it weighed on him, that he was keeping it all inside,” says his wife, Dede, to whom he has been married for 62 years. “He just never talked about his past. I could see it boiling inside him.”

His reluctance was complicated. He did not want people to pity him. And if he was successful, he wanted it to be because of who he was, not because of what had happened to him. Sitting in a chair in the small but well-stocked library of his museum, he explains how, when someone noticed his accent and asked where he was from, he would turn the question back on them: “Where do you think I’m from?” If they answered “New York,” he would reply, “Yes, exactly! New York.” If they guessed “Germany,” he would say, “Germany, yes, how did you figure that out?” and smile.

His wife, Dede, a trained social worker, finally sat down with him one evening by a crackling fire. “I asked him to tell me his story,” she recalls. “It seemed to break through the barrier. Once he said it out loud, he was able to open up to others.”

It started slowly. In 1977, at a meeting of the Wilmette, Illinois Rotary Club, Harris met a fellow member, Rabbi William Frankel, who had grown up in Vienna. Like Harris, Frankel had fled from the Nazis, but he had turned his past into a platform for activism. He had marched with Reverend Martin Luther King Jr. during the U.S. civil rights movement in the 1960s and played a significant role in Catholic-Jewish reconciliation. He befriended Harris and convinced him that he owed it to future generations to share his story.

At the same time, some neo-Nazis were planning a demonstration in Skokie, a Chicago suburb with a large Jewish population, including many Holocaust survivors. Although a lengthy legal battle ultimately prevented the march, activists like Rabbi Frankel believed that silence and inaction were no longer options. Hoping that evil would simply fade away had never been a wise strategy.

Around that time, Arthur Butz, an associate professor of electrical engineering at Northwestern University in Evanston—just ten miles from Harris’s home—published a book claiming the Holocaust was a myth perpetuated by the Allies and Zionists. Frankel called Harris, outraged by the book. “Sam, I know this is hard for you, but it’s time for you to speak up,” Frankel urged.

In Frankel’s basement, the rabbi interviewed Harris on camera. “And that was the first time I was really able to speak,” Harris says. “I told myself: I will never do this again. But then he showed the video to everyone in the community. The room was full. Then he passed it on to other rabbis. It all happened because of Rotary.”
More than 30 years after the events, Harris finally began to talk about his once-unspeakable past.
“I asked him to tell me his story. It seemed to break through the barrier. Once he said it out loud, he was able to open up to others.”

As a living bridge from that lost pre-war Jewish world—through one of humanity’s darkest chapters and into the present—Harris is today a vibrant presence. Yes, he moves with a walker, but he also rolls into the lobby of the Illinois Holocaust Museum & Education Center in a fresh blue shirt, smiling and cracking jokes, his wife by his side. He passes by a display of Jewish faces—one of them his own. Many of those faces are no longer here. But he is.
“I did not tell you this to weaken you,” reads a line woven through the portraits, “but to strengthen you.”

The youngest of seven children, Harris was born in 1935 as Szlamek Rzeznik in Dęblin, Poland. His father was a sofer, a scribe who wrote and restored Torah scrolls and other sacred texts. At four years old, he was just old enough to remember the final moments of normalcy—Sabbath dinners, weekend visits to his grandparents by horse-drawn carriage. A last taste of a normal life, the sweetness of love and laughter, which helped him endure what was to come.
It was September 1939.

“I clearly remember sitting at lunch,” he says. “We heard noises in the sky. The German Luftwaffe flew in to destroy the Polish Air Force. Dęblin had an airfield. Soon, these planes also attacked the people. That was the first time I saw death.”

The Nazis quickly occupied Dęblin and forced Jewish families into a ghetto. Food became scarce. Harris’s older sister, Rosa, was forced to work at the airfield’s labor camp and brought back what little food she could for the family.
One day in 1942, trucks carrying Nazi soldiers entered the ghetto. Armed with rifles and bayonets, they drove the Jews onto the town’s marketplace.
“If someone refused or didn’t move fast enough, the soldiers stabbed or shot them,” Harris says.

Nearby, cattle cars waited to transport the Jews to an extermination camp. Harris believes their destination was the camp near the village of Treblinka in eastern Poland, where an estimated 700,000 to 900,000 Jews were murdered.

“I stood in line with my father, who held my hand,” says Harris. “Since I was so small, I could only see people’s legs and hear gunshots. All I could do was look up at the sky. I felt like a guardian angel was there to protect me.”

Harris’s father pushed the boy out of the line and ordered him to run. Fortunately, the Nazi soldiers didn’t notice, and he ran away. He saw his sister Sara hiding behind some bricks. The two children crouched and silently watched as their parents, cousins, brothers, sisters, and neighbors were taken toward the cattle cars. That was the last time Harris saw them.

Harris and Sara later reunited with their older sister Rosa. Constantly on the run, Rosa smuggled them into the forced labor camp. Harris was too young to work and would have been shot if discovered. His job was to hide.

Fate also played a role. Another prisoner, an Austrian Jew named Hermann Wenkart, recognized a Nazi soldier he had served with in the Austrian army during World War I. The soldier, Eduard Bromofsky, had been wounded in battle, and Wenkart had saved his life by carrying him to safety. Bromofsky introduced his savior to his SS comrades, who, impressed by Wenkart’s bravery, appointed him as the camp elder.

In 1944, as the Nazi troops retreated from the advancing Russians, they relocated Jewish forced laborers to a concentration camp near Częstochowa in southern Poland to produce ammunition for the front. Before leaving, Wenkart ensured that an officer signed a letter requesting that his daughter, Ruthi, and other arriving children be spared.
Meanwhile, Harris and his sisters arrived in Częstochowa, where a guard quickly tried to push Harris aside.
“My sister Rosa cried, and I didn’t want to leave,” he recalls. “Well, the guard kicked me right in my little chest.”
The guard grabbed Harris and took him to a room with four other children, all scheduled to be shot the next day. One of those children was Ruthi.

Wenkart made sure the letter was given to a Nazi officer in the camp. At first, the officer agreed only to spare Ruthi. Wenkart shook his head and insisted, “Either all or none.” The Nazi officer finally relented and let the children stay.
During the day, Harris lived in the shadows. He slept in a women’s barracks and constantly wet the bed out of fear of going to the latrine, where prisoners who had tried to escape were hanged.
“My bedding was a straw sack, and it started to rot,” he says. “I lay on it and got fleas and lice. They were everywhere, in my ears, and my whole body was pink.”

In January 1945, the Soviet army liberated the camp in Częstochowa. Harris was so hungry that when a fellow prisoner brought him a lump of butter from the kitchen, he ate the whole piece and got sick.

With rags for shoes, Harris hitchhiked with other survivors back to Dęblin, but their homes had been occupied by civilians. Harris and Sara were then placed in an orphanage after Rosa married a Jewish man from Vienna. In 1946, Rosa managed to smuggle Sam and Sara out of Poland to Austria and arranged for them to be adopted in the United States. After a stormy Atlantic crossing, they arrived in New York in 1947.
“The three words I learned on the ship were ‘yes,’ ‘no,’ and ‘Coca-Cola,’” Harris recalls.

What was America like for a boy who had grown up in a small Polish town and spent the last five years in one horrific camp after another? “I chewed gum,” he says. “We had white bread. I remember seeing Americans sitting with their feet on their desks, leaning back, eating steak. I thought that was pretty great.”
The two siblings were adopted by different families. “Nobody wanted two children from the concentration camps,” he notes.
He was adopted by the Harris family and moved to Northbrook, while Sara lived with a family in Chicago.

Growing up, he just wanted to “be an American boy.” He joined every club, became class president, and was popular among his peers, who had no idea what he had been through. “I had made up my mind to build a wall around my head, a concrete wall, and not talk about it.”
That was during the day when he could control his thoughts. But at night, disturbing dreams broke through that wall. “My adoptive mother would sit with me, crying, when I screamed,” he says.

Harris went to college and built a successful career in insurance. As his business grew, he joined Rotary in 1967. A cousin, a member of the first Rotary Club in Chicago, took him to a meeting. “I really liked it,” Harris says, “and I’ve been a Rotarian ever since.”

In 1978, NBC aired the miniseries Holocaust over four nights. More than 100 million Americans watched it, and it was credited with making the term “Holocaust” widely recognized. That same year, President Jimmy Carter established a commission that led to the creation of the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington, D.C. What would later become the Illinois Holocaust Museum also had its beginnings. Harris calls it “a little museum,” which opened in 1984 in a storefront in Skokie. Donations trickled in. In 2006, the Glencoe Public Library donated records from the Nuremberg Trials.

Harris wrote his story in a book, Sammy: Child Survivor of the Holocaust. But by the 1990s, he realized that a storefront—and even his book—would not be enough. But how do you build a museum? Where do you start?

Being an active Rotarian was key. “I was at a roundtable discussion with a group of Rotarians,” Harris says. “I said, ‘Hey, I volunteered last night to build a museum.’ Everyone wanted to be involved. One person said they would help me raise money. Another offered to find the land, and someone else would handle the architecture. My first committee was in Rotary. They all volunteered to work with me.”
As the museum neared completion, Harris emphasized the importance of making it more than just a memorial—it had to be a living part of the community.
“The purpose of the museum is to educate people,” Harris says. “As we move further away from World War II… gas chambers, killing all those people—people don’t know about it anymore.”

A recent survey of Americans under 40 found that 12 percent had never heard the word “Holocaust.” Nearly half couldn’t name a concentration camp, and 63 percent didn’t know how many Jews were murdered.
In the late 1980s, Harris joined a successful campaign that required Illinois public schools to teach Holocaust history.
Harris’s cracked leather belt is displayed in the museum. “This belt is the only thing I have left from the concentration camps,” he says.
The museum opened in 2009 in a stark gray and white building designed by renowned architect Stanley Tigerman, symbolizing a journey from darkness to light.
Harris remains deeply involved in youth outreach through Rotary. “I don’t want this brutal tragedy to happen to other children just because of their race,” he says.
International Holocaust Remembrance Day is January 27, the anniversary of the liberation of Auschwitz-Birkenau.
